The valuable educators throughout this highly productive first-year program, along with Ms. Hockstein, were Dr. Sandra Lynch, the Transition Coordinator for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District, and Job Coaches and Teachers Andrew Ciliberto, Suzanne Percival Kathleen Hands, and Kristin Winchock Ms. Hockstein closed the book on this terrific program for the 2024-2025 school year. “We are extremely proud of the LionHearts' achievements and look forward to a new group of Bridgewater-Raritan High School students /LionHearts in the fall.”

title: Retirement will present a new chapter in the (Met) life of Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Janine Kearney  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Retirement will present a new chapter in the (Met) life of Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Janine Kearney Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District June 10, 2025 Janine Kearney did not hesitate to answer this question. So, what are you going to do with your retirement? “I plan to travel, attend music festivals, and spend time with my dog, Leo. We will go for many walks,’ Ms. Kearney revealed when she bids farewell on June 19 to Bridgewater-Raritan High School after 26 years teaching Chemistry. She and her husband, Jim, will also make their annual fall trips to MetLife Stadium in the Meadowlands, home of the New York Football Giants. They are the owners of two season tickets to the NFL team at MetLife Stadium. “We are looking forward to them winning some games at some point in the future,” Ms. Kearney added. And then she happily brought up one more important item in her answer about retirement. “I'm going to sleep in.” Yes, the bell sounds early at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, and now Ms. Kearney doesn't have to set her alarm clock anymore in her home in East Amwell Township. Ms.

title: Retirement will present a new chapter in the (Met) life of Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Janine Kearney  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ds early at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, and now Ms. Kearney doesn't have to set her alarm clock anymore in her home in East Amwell Township. Ms. Kearney spent 20 of her 26 years teaching AP Chemistry at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, a period that she deeply cherished. “It was a pleasure to teach the best and brightest students Bridgewater-Raritan had to offer,” Ms. Kearney declared. Before she came to Bridgewater-Raritan, Ms. Kearney taught for four years at Bernards High School and three years previously at Southern Regional High School in Manahawkin. She also enjoyed her role as the Advisor to the New Jersey Science League team, a position she served in for 15 years at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. “That was a lot of fun. We had plenty of students win awards, and some years we had students and teams finish No. 1 in the state,” she said. “Our Biology team ended up No. 1 in the competition this year.” She will leave 600 Garretson Road with fond memories. “ It was a pleasure to work with so many talented teachers and our wonderful supervisor, Mike Herbst,” Ms. Kearney claimed. According to Mr.

title: Retirement will present a new chapter in the (Met) life of Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Janine Kearney  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: emories. “ It was a pleasure to work with so many talented teachers and our wonderful supervisor, Mike Herbst,” Ms. Kearney claimed. According to Mr. Herbst, the Supervisor of Science Grades 6-12 for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District, the feeling was mutual. "Janine has always been a shining example to her colleagues and students alike. She approached every challenge with grace, integrity, and unwavering commitment,” Mr. Herbst stated. “Beyond her expertise, it was her genuine care for every student that truly made a difference—always patient, understanding, and encouraging, Janine helped countless young minds grow not just academically but as individuals.” Mr. Herbst continued with his touching sendoff. “Janine's dedication to teaching has been inspiring. She has poured her heart into each workday, shaping the future one student at a time. Janine Kearney's passion for education and her belief in the potential of each student have left an indelible mark on our community." Ms. Kearney shared a final thought. “I will miss it.” But she’ll not miss that alarm clock sounding off each morning.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School DECA team excelled at national challenge in Atlanta | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School DECA team excelled at national challenge in Atlanta Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District June 9, 2025 Bridgewater-Raritan High School students who are members of DECA, Saketh Bongu, Manas Gumedelli, Alexander Kellam, and Neel Wakde, placed fifth nationally when they represented New Jersey in the highly competitive Adam Smith Division of the National Economics Challenge (NEC) held in Atlanta, Georgia, from May 28–30. Guided by their DECA Advisor, Shilpa Wakde, the team competed against the best high school economics students from across the country. According to Ms. Wakde, the Adam Smith Division is the most rigorous level of the National Economics Challenge, reserved for students taking AP, IB, or honors-level economics courses. With sharp analytical thinking, deep content knowledge, and excellent teamwork, our students rose to the challenge with confidence and poise. After advancing through both regional and state competitions, the Bridgewater-Raritan High School team achieved its extraordinary fifth-place finish.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School DECA team excelled at national challenge in Atlanta | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: After advancing through both regional and state competitions, the Bridgewater-Raritan High School team achieved its extraordinary fifth-place finish. That high placement established Bridgewater-Raritan High School as one of the top economics teams in the United States. “This remarkable accomplishment reflects not only the students’ hard work and academic excellence but also the strength of the DECA and economics programs at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. We are incredibly proud of Saketh, Manas, Alexander, and Neel for representing our school and state on the national stage with such distinction,” said Ms. Wakde, who is a French Teacher and also serves as the Co-Advisor of the French Honor Society at the high school She concluded: “Congratulations to Saketh, Manas, Alexander, and Neel on this remarkable achievement!”

title: Students were rightfully honored when the Senior Awards Night Ceremony was staged at Bridgewater-Raritan High School | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Students were rightfully honored when the Senior Awards Night Ceremony was staged at Bridgewater-Raritan High School Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District June 6, 2025 Before they will receive their diplomas, students in the Class of 2025 were the recipients of scholarships for their achievements at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. These accomplished seniors were highlighted under the bright lights in the Bridgewater-Raritan High School auditorium on the evening of Wednesday, June 4. The celebration was the Senior Awards Night Ceremony, Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s annual tradition to honor particular graduating students with scholarships. Family, friends, high school officials and staff, as well as representatives from various high school departments, clubs and programs and local organizations, gathered to pay tribute to the seniors whose scholastic careers will come to a close on June 19, the date of the 2025 Commencement Exercise at Rutgers University. The main hosts for this elite ceremony were Bridgewater-Raritan High School Principal Dan Hemberger and Alan Iachini, the Supervisor of School Counseling.

title: Students were rightfully honored when the Senior Awards Night Ceremony was staged at Bridgewater-Raritan High School | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ain hosts for this elite ceremony were Bridgewater-Raritan High School Principal Dan Hemberger and Alan Iachini, the Supervisor of School Counseling. Also participating in the presentations were the high school’s Assistant Principals, Priscilla Aniegbuna, Tracee Cobbs, Adam Leaman and Damian Storey, and high school and school district staff and other invited donors. The Counseling department’s dedicated Administrative Assistant, Paula Clark, was also a major contributor to the evening festivities. Principal Hemberger cherishes this annual award event that caps the school year. “Senior Awards Night is often the most rewarding evening of the year. It's a signature event for our community and the culmination of four years of achievement for our students. It was, once again, a joyous occasion,” Principal Hemberger said. He concluded: “Lots of smiles!” Click on the link here to view all of the student names and the awards that they all received. https://drive.google.com/file/d/1KXG6746ULJGeplqdUHepsERPLx1_GVa1/view?usp=sharing

title: Class-act Sandy Baranowski has waved goodbye to a memorable career at Bridgewater-Raritan High School  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Class-act Sandy Baranowski has waved goodbye to a memorable career at Bridgewater-Raritan High School Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District June 6, 2025 Sandy Baranowski’s legendary career as Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s head softball coach came to an end on Wednesday, June 4 After 28 years and four different decades in charge, it was therefore only fitting that her team went out with a fight against the reigning Group 4 state champion Watchung Hills Regional High School deep in the state sectional tournament. The final scoreboard did not reflect the extraordinary performance of the Panthers under their retiring leader, who has served as a health and physical education teacher at Bridgewater-Raritan High School since 1993. After advancing to the state sectional semifinals for the first time in eight years, fourth-seeded Bridgewater-Raritan (15-8) found itself in a scoreless draw with two outs in the bottom of the third inning at top-seeded Watchung Hills (21-6), ranked No. 12 in New Jersey.

title: Class-act Sandy Baranowski has waved goodbye to a memorable career at Bridgewater-Raritan High School  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: 8) found itself in a scoreless draw with two outs in the bottom of the third inning at top-seeded Watchung Hills (21-6), ranked No. 12 in New Jersey. While the Panther outfield collected six of the game’s eight outs up to that moment, Watchung Hills capitalized on a pair of miscues by Bridgewater-Raritan to break through for two runs and take the lead for good. To cap off her victory on the circle on a day when she collected career strikeout No. 400, junior pitcher Riley Bobrowski fired a no-hitter with 12 strikeouts and five walks for Watchung Hills, which pulled away for a 10-0 victory in six innings to eliminate Bridgewater-Raritan in theNJSIAA North New Jersey, Section 2, Group 4, state sectional semifinals on June 4 in Warren. “It was a tremendous season,” reflected Coach Baranowski after the team’s defeat and her final game as head coach. “I love coaching these girls because they are just super fun. Sometimes, the better team wins.” “Today, that’s what it was.” Watchung Hills players and coaching staff surrounded the retiring Baranowski with applause and praise during post-game handshakes.

title: Class-act Sandy Baranowski has waved goodbye to a memorable career at Bridgewater-Raritan High School  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: at’s what it was.” Watchung Hills players and coaching staff surrounded the retiring Baranowski with applause and praise during post-game handshakes. As the Panthers were also about to depart the field for their team bus, a member of the Watchung Hills team delivered Coach Baranowski what appeared to be the game ball as a token of appreciation for the everlasting impact she had as Bridgewater-Raritan’s leader. “This team is probably going to the state finals,” praised Coach Baranowski of Watchung Hills. With its defeat, Bridgewater-Raritan (15-8) completed its final season under the leadership of Baranowski with its best record and deepest state tournament run since 2017, when the Panthers last won the Somerset County Softball Tournament and reached the state sectional finals. Coach Baranowski will end her run as the Bridgewater-Raritan skipper with 365 victories, which is tied for second in Somerset County softball history with former Bound Brook High School head coach Steve Kania. Many Bridgewater-Raritan supporters surrounded all ends of the Watchung Hills field during the postgame while Baranowski was huddled around her team just behind first base in right field.

title: Class-act Sandy Baranowski has waved goodbye to a memorable career at Bridgewater-Raritan High School  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: rrounded all ends of the Watchung Hills field during the postgame while Baranowski was huddled around her team just behind first base in right field. While she made her impact as the softball coach, Coach Baranowski coached junior varsity field hockey (1993-94), junior varsity boys’ volleyball (1993-97) and junior varsity girls’ basketball (1993-97). She also served as the Advisor to the Fuel Up To Play 60 club at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. “I’m at ease with my decision,” revealed Coach Baranowski, who, in her upcoming retirement, will always be remembered by those within and beyond the Bridgewater-Raritan High School community for her unmatchable passion, work ethic, and enthusiasm. So, how did she soak in everything when she left that softball field for the final time on June 4? “Indescribable,” Coach Baranowski concluded.

title: The Bridgewater-Raritan High School Band deserved to take a bow for a spectacular performance  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content The Bridgewater-Raritan High School Band deserved to take a bow for a spectacular performance Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District June 5, 2025 A school year that featured the splendid sounds of concerts—both vocal and instrumental—at Bridgewater-Raritan High School was capped on the night of Thursday, May 29. It was on that evening when the Bridgewater-Raritan High School Bands presented their Spring Concert before an attentive and enjoyable audience that jammed into the high school auditorium. The Spring Band Concert followed a spectacular Bridgewater-Raritan High School concert lineup in the spring, when the Choral Concert was sung on Tuesday, May 27, and the Orchestra Concert was played on Tuesday, May 20. Matt McCarthy, the guiding force behind what is arguably the finest Visual and Performing Arts program in all of New Jersey in his role as the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District Supervisor of Arts Education, applauded the concerts throughout the entire year when he spoke about the band’s performance.

title: The Bridgewater-Raritan High School Band deserved to take a bow for a spectacular performance  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Regional School District Supervisor of Arts Education, applauded the concerts throughout the entire year when he spoke about the band’s performance. “The Bridgewater-Raritan High School Band program continues to be a cornerstone of our district’s commitment to excellence in arts education,” Mr. McCarthy claimed. He shared more of his positive beliefs. “Bridgewater-Raritan has such a tight-knit and vibrant arts community that really allows these young musicians to thrive. In each number, these bands showed how the whole is truly greater than the sum of its parts. You could hear the months of preparation and feel the genuine connection between the performers - it was really something special,” Mr. McCarthy said. The Band Concert delivered a sound presentation from the three groups—the Concert Band, the Symphonic Band, and the Wind Ensemble. The students played under the guidance of the high school’s two devoted Band Directors, Nick Mossa and Dr. Tom Bourgault. Also contributing during this productive concert was Student Teacher Franklin Hansen, who came through with a fine job conducting the Concert Band.

Mr. Mossa added: “We are especially proud of our graduating seniors who will be missed dearly!" Mr. McCarthy credited the leadership for the success of the Bridgewater-Raritan High School Band program. “A huge thank you to Dr. Bourgault, Mr. Mossa, and Student Teacher Franklin Hansen for their incredible dedication. Their commitment to developing complete musicians really shows. I’m proud to have such passionate educators at the conductor’s podium,” he said. title: Eight Bridgewater-Raritan High School Latin students received honors in national exam  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Eight Bridgewater-Raritan High School Latin students received honors in national exam Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District June 4, 2025 Eight Bridgewater-Raritan High School students passed their exam with flying colors. A total of 15 Bridgewater-Raritan High School students in Latin II A took the National Medusa Mythology Exam in March. And eight were recently notified that they won certificates or medals! The eight students who achieved the honors are: Hansika Nakkina, Diya Patel and Anna Dolenjashvili won cum laude certificates. Brooke Engstrom and Anish Gannamaneni won magna cum laude ribbons. Karthi Muthu, Katelyn Alger and Michael Reeves won maxima cum laude silver medals. “The whole class worked very hard to prepare for this difficult exam,” said Elizabeth Farshtey, a World Language Teacher at the high school. She added: “ I am so proud of them! “

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Brenda Hockstein and Jessica Kaiser honored as Governor's Award winners at BRRSD Staff Recognition Reception  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Brenda Hockstein and Jessica Kaiser honored as Governor's Award winners at BRRSD Staff Recognition Reception Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 7, 2025 Staff members who practice devotion and care daily throughout the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District were honored when the Staff Recognition Reception was staged on Tuesday, May 6. The annual affair, which is sponsored by the Bridgewater-Raritan Education Association (BREA), drew a crowd of an estimated 190, which included school district administrators, board of education members, school staff, and family and friends of the honorees, to the Somerville Elks Lodge. The 22 honorees represented the 11 schools in the district—11 teachers were recognized as the Governor’s Educator of the Year, and 11 staff members were cited as the Governor’s Educational Services Professional. The after-school ceremony was kicked off with a welcome from Laura Kress, the President of the BREA. Superintendent Bob Beers followed with his speech that featured his admiration of the 22 award winners and the dedication and commitment that each produces in their valuable positions.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Brenda Hockstein and Jessica Kaiser honored as Governor's Award winners at BRRSD Staff Recognition Reception  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School

Each principal of the 11 schools then spoke of the exploits and efforts of their recipients, who stood up front at the podium and joined their principal during each of the individual school presentations. Jessica Kaiser, a School Counselor who was Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s Educational Services Professional Award winner, could not attend the ceremony due to a prior commitment at the high school. Gifts from the BREA were then handed out to each honoree by Board of Education member Rebecca Hassouna and Kelly Kokotajlo, the 2nd Vice President of the BREA and a 2nd Grade Teacher at the Van Holten Primary School. Here are the Governor’s Educator of the Year Award and the Governor's Educational Services Award recipients.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School Latin students earned high grades on the National Exam  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School Latin students earned high grades on the National Exam Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 6, 2025 The grades are in. And Bridgewater-Raritan High School Latin students passed with high marks. According to Lucinda Jaffe and Elizabeth Farshtey, the Bridgewater-Raritan High School Latin Teachers, a total of 87 students participated in the National Latin Exam —-and 71 received an award. Here are the results overall: 26 students earned Gold Summa Cum Laude 21 students earned Silver Maxima Cum Laude 17 students earned Magna Cum Laude 7 students earned Cum Laude 3 of the Gold Summa Cum Laude students also earned perfect scores Among the recipients were Julien Narcisso and Ria Devgan, who each were honored with Maxima Cum Laude and Mihika Tare was awarded a Magna Cum Laude.

title: Italian Culture Night celebrated at Bridgewater-Raritan High School | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Italian Culture Night celebrated at Bridgewater-Raritan High School Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 5, 2025 The culture of Italy was celebrated in fine form on Friday, April 25. On that day, the Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s Italian Honor Society served as the host to its Italian Culture Night at the high school cafeteria. According to the Italian Honor Society Advisors, Giuseppina Bologno and Giuseppina DiMartino, “ this vibrant celebration was a collaborative effort between members of the Italian Honor Society, students, and faculty from the Italian language program. The event welcomed a large and enthusiastic crowd of students, educators, parents, and grandparents, all gathered to enjoy an evening of rich cultural traditions and community engagement.” Ms. Bologno and Ms. DiMartino continued with their summary. “At the heart of the celebration was a hallmark of Italian culture: cuisine. Guests were treated to an array of traditional Italian dishes, including pizza, pasta, and an assortment of authentic snacks and desserts. Beyond the food, the evening featured interactive and entertaining activities.

title: Italian Culture Night celebrated at Bridgewater-Raritan High School | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ding pizza, pasta, and an assortment of authentic snacks and desserts. Beyond the food, the evening featured interactive and entertaining activities. A highlight of the night was the Kahoot trivia games, led by Italian Honor Society members. The Kahoot tested participants' knowledge of Italian culture, history, and general trivia, sparking lively competition and showcasing the students’ enthusiasm and dedication. The arts were also prominently featured. The event opened with a live performance of Italian classical music by Edha Gupta, setting a wonderful and refined tone for the event. The energy lifted as students Ria Mukherji and Marisa Scott led the audience in the traditional Tarantella dance, inviting everyone to share in the joyous spirit of Italian folk culture. Italian Culture Night was more than an evening of entertainment; it was a meaningful celebration of community and shared heritage.

Their collective efforts ensured a memorable and enriching experience for all.”

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School National Arts Honor Society enjoyed Spring Break at Disney World  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School National Arts Honor Society enjoyed Spring Break at Disney World Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 29, 2025 Bridgewater-Raritan High School students who are members are members of the National Arts Honor Society (NAHS) spent four days during Spring Break at the Walt Disney World Resort in Florida. Adults, including Sarah Nassan, the Advisor of the National Arts Honor Society and an Art Teacher at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, accompanied the 27 NAHS students. Marlene Morales, a Bridgewater-Raritan High School junior and the Historian for the NAHS, presented the following first-hand summary of this four-day trip to Florida. For the first time in the history of the Bridgewater Raritan High School National Arts Honor Society (NAHS), students embarked on an unforgettable four-day adventure to Disney World during Spring Break. The announcement of this exciting trip for the 2025 school year came as a delightful surprise from high school Art Teacher Sarah Nasson at the 2024 banquet, sparking enthusiasm and anticipation among the students. Ms.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School National Arts Honor Society enjoyed Spring Break at Disney World  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: as a delightful surprise from high school Art Teacher Sarah Nasson at the 2024 banquet, sparking enthusiasm and anticipation among the students. Ms. Nasson planned this trip for the dedicated members of NAHS, whose work inspired her to find an experience to give back to the hard-working and deserving students. From the outset of the 2024-2025 school year, the club members demonstrated remarkable dedication and creativity in their fundraising efforts. They organized vibrant paint parties, hosted engaging school events, and rallied community support, successfully raising funds not only for essential art supplies but also for their eagerly awaited Disney journey. The adventure began early on Sunday, April 13, as the NAHS members set off for Orlando, Florida, aiming to start the journey in Disney’s Hollywood Studios. The 27 students, brimming with excitement, divided into groups to explore the park's thrilling attractions. The day culminated in the mesmerizing 'Fantasmic!' show, leaving everyone in awe. The second day brought the students to the enchanting 'Animal Kingdom,' where they participated in an 'Immersive Storytelling' workshop.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School National Arts Honor Society enjoyed Spring Break at Disney World  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: yone in awe. The second day brought the students to the enchanting 'Animal Kingdom,' where they participated in an 'Immersive Storytelling' workshop. Guided by a Disney Imagineer, the students explored the park with a unique perspective, learning about the imaginative process behind the design of each attraction. They discovered how every detail, down to the design of the garbage bins, contributes to the overall narrative of the park. This hands-on experience revealed the meticulous attention to detail that Disney employs to create an immersive storytelling environment. At the end of the tour, students went backstage where they had the opportunity to create a story for their own park. They broke into groups focusing on costume design, park theme and color, entertainment within their theme, and a menu with interesting relevance to their topic. In the end, they presented their imaginative creations to Disney Imagineers, receiving valuable feedback and insights. On the third day, the group ventured to EPCOT for the 'Visual Art of Disney Parks' workshop. Here, they had the unique opportunity to reimagine the iconic 'Tree of Life' in their own artistic styles.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School National Arts Honor Society enjoyed Spring Break at Disney World  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: the 'Visual Art of Disney Parks' workshop. Here, they had the unique opportunity to reimagine the iconic 'Tree of Life' in their own artistic styles. Under the guidance of one of Disney's guest artists, who not only contributed paintings displayed in EPCOT but also had other notable art achievements with Disney, the students unleashed their creativity. After a discussion on how the Tree of Life was made and the ideas that went into the original park attraction, the students spent an hour drawing, guided by the Disney artist, and created their own artworks of the tree. T The rest of the day was spent exploring the park, culminating in the breathtaking 'Luminous: The Symphony of Us' show, a celebration of light and music. The final day at Disney was spent in the magical 'Magic Kingdom' park, where the students engaged in the 'Theme Park Design' workshop. This hands-on activity challenged them to conceptualize their own theme parks, focusing on attractions, construction, and essential features. Armed with clipboards, the students toured the park, sketching different park ideas at each location. At the end of the tour, they went backstage to bring their ideas to life.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School National Arts Honor Society enjoyed Spring Break at Disney World  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: students toured the park, sketching different park ideas at each location. At the end of the tour, they went backstage to bring their ideas to life. Using building materials, they created 3D models of the parks they designed and presented them to the Disney Imagineers. The rest of the day was spent enjoying the park, and as the sun set, they gathered to watch the enchanting 'Happily Ever After' show, a fitting finale to their Disney adventure. Students finally returned home with cherished memories, newfound inspiration, and a deeper appreciation for the art of storytelling. Ms. Nasson was proud of the students' work throughout the trip, and of the creativity and dedication that the Bridgewater-Raritan High School students were able to put forth on this amazing trip.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School girls’ 4x100 relay track team set school record at famed Penn Relays; Watch video of the race | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School girls’ 4x100 relay track team set school record at famed Penn Relays; Watch video of the race Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 27, 2025 A new Bridgewater-Raritan High School record in girls’ track and field was accomplished on the biggest stage of the sport. Bridgewater-Raritan’s 4x100-meter relay team, which was comprised of senior Liya Medallon, senior Stacey Yang, sophomore Shelby Stevens and sophomore Meera Buddhikot, ran a time of 50.01 to establish a school record at the prestigious Penn Relays on Friday, April 25, at legendary Franklin Field in Philadelphia. Their performance at this famed worldwide track and field event broke the Bridgewater-Raritan record of 50.28 that was set 15 years ago by Sherry Oller, Thanida Sersuwan, Morgan Monroe, and Julia Murray. “They had their sights on the record for a while now. Their hard work and commitment have really paid off,” said Adam Pyle, Bridgewater-Raritan's girls’ track and field coach.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District Security Team educated high school juniors that a crosswalk is smarter than a jaywalk | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District Security Team educated high school juniors that a crosswalk is smarter than a jaywalk Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 24, 2025 A crosswalk is much more sensible—and safe— than a jaywalk. That was the message that the highly dedicated Security Team for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District shared when the officers successfully completed a series of presentations for the Class of 2026 at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. Over the course of the program, the Security Team officers engaged with 17 Health classes of junior students. According to the Security Team, the officers delivered an important message about the dangers of jaywalking and the consequences that can result from it. During each class session, students were encouraged to use the designated crosswalks when crossing Garretson Road or any other busy streets near the high school campus. The Security Team emphasized that using crosswalks is the safest option.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District Security Team educated high school juniors that a crosswalk is smarter than a jaywalk | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ossing Garretson Road or any other busy streets near the high school campus. The Security Team emphasized that using crosswalks is the safest option. According to the Security Team, which is under the guidance of Coordinator Mike Voorhees and Assistant Coordinator Ted Lewis, the initiative would not have been possible without the collaborative efforts of school staff and students who developed the curriculum, created a flyer to include in morning announcements, and helped organize the logistics of the campaign. Their support ensured that the message was clear, consistent, and impactful across all participating classes. In conclusion, the Security Team stated, “By prioritizing education and awareness, the Bridgewater-Raritan School District Security Team hopes to foster a culture of safety and responsibility among students and significantly reduce the risks associated with jaywalking.”

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School junior Angela Zhou is an award-winning artist | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School junior Angela Zhou is an award-winning artist Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 17, 2025 A paintbrush, revealed Angela Zhou, is a relaxing tool. Angela, who is a junior at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, has been painting since she was six years old. “It helps take the stress out of school,” Angela admitted. That source of relief has resulted in a collection of elite awards for Angela. Angela was notified in late March that she was honored with the prestigious 2025 National Scholastic Award, earning both a Gold Medal and the American Vision Award. According to the Alliance for Young Artists & Writers, the Scholastic Art & Writing Awards are the longest-running and most prestigious recognition program for creative teens in the United States. The Gold Medal is the highest honor a student can receive, with less than one percent of all students submitting works earning this award. Only 150 out of over 300,000 submissions receive the American Vision Award.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School junior Angela Zhou is an award-winning artist | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ess than one percent of all students submitting works earning this award. Only 150 out of over 300,000 submissions receive the American Vision Award. According to its website, the Alliance for Young Artists & Writers, a nonprofit organization, identifies teenagers with exceptional artistic and literary talent and brings their remarkable work to a national audience through The Scholastic Art & Writing Awards. The Awards have an impressive legacy dating to 1923. In addition to her Scholastic Award, Angela also placed first in the Teen Category of the 17th International ARC Salon Competition, the world’s largest and most prestigious competition dedicated to 21st-century realism. She was awarded a $2,500 prize for her exceptional work. She has been an artist since “I was six years old.” “I also like to listen to music while I paint,” Angela revealed. Her award-winning painting, titled “Laboratory Study,” was submitted to the Alliance for Young Artists & Writers following the studio's recommendation, where she crafted her fine artworks at The Art Academy in Hillsborough. There, she works closely with the founder of The Art Academy, Kevin Murphy.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School junior Angela Zhou is an award-winning artist | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: , where she crafted her fine artworks at The Art Academy in Hillsborough. There, she works closely with the founder of The Art Academy, Kevin Murphy. Growing up, she won numerous awards for her paintings, designed a book cover, and illustrated three children’s books. Angela, who studied Digital Art during her first year at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, is on track to be a student in the AP Art and Design class this coming fall at the high school. She will continue to thrive in her work in that course next school year while preparing for college. She hopes to enroll at the University of Pennsylvania and study business while continuing to paint. Her painting commitment and success have not prevented her from being involved in extracurricular activities at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. She is the Treasurer for the Student Council, the Marketing Director for the “Lunch Buddy” club, a member of the Model United Nations, the French Honor Society, and the Key Club. At the same time, she runs for the track and field team and also the cross-country squad.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School sophomore Yashvi Shah competed at the famed Augusta National Golf Club  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School sophomore Yashvi Shah competed at the famed Augusta National Golf Club Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 11, 2025 The Masters, the prestigious golf tournament, and the first of the four majors that are played each year, began on April 10 at the famous Augusta National Golf Club in Georgia. A week before the best golfers in the world teed off for the tournament that will run until Sunday, April 13, a Bridgewater-Raritan High School sophomore played the fairways and the greens at the pristine course. Yashvi Shah, a member of the Bridgewater-Raritan High School girls’ golf team, competed in the 11th Drive, Chip, and Putt National Finals held on Sunday, April 6. She had qualified at a subregional tournament earlier in 2024 to compete in this national event that was broadcast live on April 6 on the Golf Channel. Yashvi placed 10th in her 14-15 age division for girls on that Sunday, when she was accompanied at Augusta National by her high school coach, Steve Kline, and her family. That day was a memorable one for her at Augusta National. “It was a great experience that I enjoyed with my family and coaches,’ Yashvi said.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School sophomore Yashvi Shah competed at the famed Augusta National Golf Club  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: family. That day was a memorable one for her at Augusta National. “It was a great experience that I enjoyed with my family and coaches,’ Yashvi said. “Playing at Augusta National was a dream come true. It was an honor to compete there. I hope in the future I will earn my way back.” Here is the official press release from Augusta National Golf Club on the tournament. Eight junior golfers – four boys and four girls – were crowned champions at the 11th Drive, Chip and Putt National Finals held today, Sunday, April 6, at Augusta National Golf Club and broadcast live on Golf Channel across North and South America. Conducted in partnership with the Masters Tournament, USGA and the PGA of America, Drive, Chip and Putt is a free youth golf development initiative for junior golfers of all skill and ability levels, aimed at sparking a lifelong interest in the game. The three-pronged competition welcomes boys and girls, ages 7-15, in four age categories, and tests the skills essential to golf: accuracy in driving, chipping and putting.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School sophomore Yashvi Shah competed at the famed Augusta National Golf Club  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: tition welcomes boys and girls, ages 7-15, in four age categories, and tests the skills essential to golf: accuracy in driving, chipping and putting. Tens of thousands of participants seized the opportunity to qualify for the event, which began with hundreds of local qualifiers at courses across the country in the summer of 2024. From there, the top finishers advanced through subregional qualifying events to one of 10 regional qualifiers at some of the nation’s most acclaimed venues and championship sites. Finally, the 80 regional winners across the four age divisions (40 boys and 40 girls) earned a trip to Augusta National Golf Club to participate in the 11th Drive, Chip and Putt National Finals The four girl champions were: Ellie Kim of Danville, Calif. (7-9); Bella Simoes of Lake Worth, Fla. (10-11); Alexandra Phung of Forest Hills, N.Y. (12-13); and Abigail Henriksen of DeForest, Wis. (14-15). The four boy champions were: Joshua Hooper of Wildwood, Mo. (7-9); Dawson Dial of Cedar Park, Texas (10-11); Hudson Justus of Gainesville, Ga. (12-13); and Anthony Wu of Mission Viejo, Calif. (14-15).

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School sophomore Yashvi Shah competed at the famed Augusta National Golf Club  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ood, Mo. (7-9); Dawson Dial of Cedar Park, Texas (10-11); Hudson Justus of Gainesville, Ga. (12-13); and Anthony Wu of Mission Viejo, Calif. (14-15). Points were awarded in three individual skill categories (driving, chipping and putting), with the winner in each skill receiving 10 points, second place receiving 9 points, and so on, all the way down to 1 point. The player with the most points following all three competitions was declared the overall winner of the age group. For the drive portion, the better of two distances was used for the score. Each golfer then took two chips; the closest cumulative distance to the hole determined the winner. Players then moved to Augusta National’s No. 18 green where they attempted two putts – from 15 and 30 feet – and used the cumulative distance from the hole to determine the score.

title: A standing ovation for the performance of Footloose by the Bridgewater-Raritan Theatre Arts | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content A standing ovation for the performance of Footloose by the Bridgewater-Raritan Theatre Arts Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 11, 2025 A performance that would rival a professional showing was staged at the Bridgewater-Raritan High School auditorium during the first week of April. It was in that theatre where the skills of the Bridgewater-Raritan High School Theatre Arts were on full exhibit. The students and staff who comprise this highly acclaimed Bridgewater-Raritan High School Theatre Arts joined forces to present the hit musical Footloose on the evenings of Thursday, April 3, and Friday, April 4, and then again at 2 p.m. and at 7 p.m. on Saturday, April 5. The audience who witnessed these shows came away impressed with how gifted the students sang and danced on stage and how the crew off stage worked their magic. The music pit crew also produced sound music, courtesy of the conductor, Dr. Tom Bourgault, the Co-Director of the Bridgewater-Raritan High School Band with Nick Mossa. One could see how well-trained and prepared the students were when playing out their roles and characters in this lively production.

She was gratified by the finished product on stage during the performances. “ I am incredibly proud of every student involved in this production and so grateful to have the opportunity to create theatre with them here at Bridgewater-Raritan High School,” claimed Ms. Diaforli-Day the Theatre and Music Teacher at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. Her tireless and energetic work with the students are well respected. The singing and dancing were phenomenal. The sets looked amazing and the band rocked,” Mr. McCarthy said. The curtain will rise again to the delight of Mr. McCarthy.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School seniors were encouraged to go out and vote  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School seniors were encouraged to go out and vote Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 7, 2025 Your voice can be heard. That was the message delivered by officials from the League of Women Voters who spoke to the senior class at Bridgewater-Raritan High School on Friday, April 4. The Bridgewater-Raritan High School High Student Council was the sponsor of this informative event that was held in the auditorium during Health classes that day. Michele Mammone, who is the Co-Advisor of the Student Council with Sarah Cleary, explained the session. “The League of Women Voters put on a presentation to encourage the seniors to register to vote. The students were engaged and interested in the presentation and were encouraged to take part of the upcoming gubernatorial vote and have a voice as young voters,” Ms. Mammone said. She added: “The League of Women Voters collected 64 completed registration forms. Many students have already registered to vote when they went for their driver's license.”

title: Giada Catanzaro connected for her 100th career goal in Bridgewater-Raritan's girls’ lacrosse victory over Phillipsburg  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Giada Catanzaro connected for her 100th career goal in Bridgewater-Raritan's girls’ lacrosse victory over Phillipsburg Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 6, 2025 When she was a freshman at Bridgewater-Raritan High School two years ago, Giada Catanzaro surged onto the high school girls’ lacrosse scene by collecting an astounding 12 points in her debut to open the season at Mount Saint Mary Academy. Perhaps this was an appropriate foreshadowing of similar results to come for the Panthers junior, who is currently committed to playing NCAA Division 1 lacrosse at Virginia Tech. While collecting that many points in one game is a difficult feat alone, Catanzaro still managed to eclipse this with 13 points against Ridge High School in the Somerset County Tournament quarterfinals last year in her sophomore season. After opening her junior campaign with 10 points in a victory at Voorhees High School on Tuesday, April 1, it was therefore not a surprise that Catanzaro clinched another big milestone in her next game 48 hours later.

title: Giada Catanzaro connected for her 100th career goal in Bridgewater-Raritan's girls’ lacrosse victory over Phillipsburg  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: rhees High School on Tuesday, April 1, it was therefore not a surprise that Catanzaro clinched another big milestone in her next game 48 hours later. Not even one minute after the game’s opening draw, Catanzaro (three goals, four assists) hit for her first goal of the game and goal No. 100 for her career as part of an eight-goal first period by Bridgewater-Raritan (2-0). Including Catanzaro, three of the Panthers’ six goal-scorers dialed up hat tricks, and Bridgewater-Raritan rolled to a 15-2 victory in its home opener against Phillipsburg High School (0-2) Thursday, April 3, at John Basilone Memorial Field in Bridgewater. Most importantly, not only did Catanzaro’s teammates hold up a congratulatory poster they made for her immediately after reaching No. 100 with 11:04 left in the first quarter. Right after the final buzzer, they delivered her a trio of ‘1’, ‘0’, and ‘0’ balloons to further commemorate her big moment. A gesture that is certainly consistent with the cornerstone ‘Family’ mantra this storied Bridgewater-Raritan girls’ lacrosse program has been built on since its inception.

This group of girls is really special.” “They do really believe in themselves as a family.” Seniors Emma Ciocon (four goals, four draw controls), Allie Prickett (four goals), and Ella Daehnke (two goals) were the remaining multi-goal scorers for Bridgewater-Raritan, which added one goal each from freshmen Sarah Hurlbut (one goal, two assists) and Ashley Ciufo (one goal). Senior Sophie Levin grabbed a team-high six draw controls, while freshman Sam Tremarco hauled in four draw controls, and junior Mia James and freshman Skye Cabatu each snatched onedraw control. The Panthers will serve as host to Watchung Hills Regional High School (1-3) at 4 p.m.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School sophomore Lucia Bolle honored with the prestigious Governor's Award in Arts Education  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School sophomore Lucia Bolle honored with the prestigious Governor's Award in Arts Education Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 3, 2025 Lucia Bolle has experienced one memorable sophomore year at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. This highly skilled artist has been honored with numerous awards for her paintings—all in just one year. Her most recent recognition may be her finest yet. Lucia was notified the week of March 31 by the Art Administrators of New Jersey (AANJ) that she was the recipient of the 2025 AANJ Visual Art Student Achievement Award. This prestigious honor is the Governor’s Award in Arts Education. Lucia’s honor in 2025 completed a hat trick for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District. Before Lucia earnd this achievement for 2025, Crystal Zhang received the Visual Art Student Achievement Award in 2023, and Sharanya Ram was the recipient in 2024. Each of those two students was a senior at Bridgewater-Raritan High School when honored. Also in 2023, Alexander Lu, a senior that year at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, was honored with the Governor's Award for All-State Orchestra.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School sophomore Lucia Bolle honored with the prestigious Governor's Award in Arts Education  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ed. Also in 2023, Alexander Lu, a senior that year at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, was honored with the Governor's Award for All-State Orchestra. According to the AANJ Executive Board, Lucia’s portfolio was selected from a pool of very talented artists from across New Jersey. The notification stated: “Our panel felt that your artwork stood out for its originality, craftsmanship, use of media, and the application of the elements and principles of art." "In conjunction with this award, you will be presented with a $200 check from our organization in recognition of this honor.” “Again, congratulations on this outstanding accomplishment. We wish you the very best in all of your future endeavors.” This year alone, Lucia received notice on March 26 that she was the recipient of two National Medals in the 2025 Scholastic Art & Writing Awards for her work called Hello, World, which received a Gold Medal, and her work titled Sorrow - The Weight Of Water, received a Gold medal as well as the New York Life award, giving her a $2,500 scholarship.

She was also accepted in March into the 2025 National Art Education Association (NAEA) National Art Honor Society (NAHS)/National Junior Art Honor Society (NJAHS) Juried Exhibition. And her artwork Nostalgia: What Once Was - is on display at the Morris Museum in Morris Township. “I am excited and very grateful for the art community that we have and the teachers who support us,” Lucia said on April 3, just a few days after she received her most recent elite honor.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School orchestra students produced a sparkling performance at the Festival of the Arts   | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School orchestra students produced a sparkling performance at the Festival of the Arts Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District April 1, 2025 The spectators who jammed inside the Vaughn Stapleton Gymnasium at Bridgewater-Raritan High School were entertained by “one of the best youth orchestra programs in the state.” The popular event on the evening of Wednesday, March 26, was the gem of the Visual and Performing Arts program at Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District—the Festival of the Arts. The Orchestra Concert, the second of three musical productions in the Festival of the Arts, was performed by students from the Eisenhower Intermediate School, the Hillside Intermediate School, the Bridgewater-Raritan Middle School and the host, Bridgewater-Raritan High School. The master of ceremonies for this gala showcase was Matt McCarthy, the dedicated Supervisor of Arts Education for the school district. He was not shy in boasting about the skilled students who occupied the spacious gym floor that evening. “The outstanding performances tonight highlighted the vitality of our orchestra community,” Mr. McCarthy said.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School orchestra students produced a sparkling performance at the Festival of the Arts   | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: d the spacious gym floor that evening. “The outstanding performances tonight highlighted the vitality of our orchestra community,” Mr. McCarthy said. “We have dynamic instructors, a comprehensive seven-year sequence, and a commitment to ensuring access to participation. It’s really very special and something worth celebrating.” Mr. McCarthy further claimed: “This is one of the best youth orchestra programs in the state.” After the National Anthem was brilliantly played by the Bridgewater-Raritan High School Symphony Orchestra, Eisenhower’s 6th Grade Orchestra played under the guidance of conductor Carrie Jendza andHillside’s 6th Grade Orchestra performed under its conductor, Thomas Lewis. The Middle School 7th Grade Orchestra, under conductor Timothy Nuzzetti, performed and those students were followed by their schoolmates, the 8th Grade Orchestra, which played under conductor Caty Butler. The three Bridgewater-Raritan High School ensembles—the Ninth Orchestra, the Concert Orchestra and the Symphony Orchestra—all provided sparkling renditions under their conductor, Hsiao-yu Lin (Sherry) Griggs.

title: A lesson in Italian history is shared with Bridgewater-Raritan High School students  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content A lesson in Italian history is shared with Bridgewater-Raritan High School students Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District March 31, 2025 Another education in Italian history was provided by a local expert to Bridgewater-Raritan High School students. The students in the Italian classes taught by Giuseppina Bologno and Giuseppina DiMartino again received the lecture of Marco Cortigiani, a distinguished member of the Florentine Organization in the United States and a resident of Basking Ridge, who paid a visit to Bridgewater-Raritan High School on Thursday, March 27. Mr. Cortigiani delivered a specialized lecture on the Calcio Storico Fiorentino, which is a combination of soccer, rugby and wrestling from 16th-century Florence. The game is still played today in costumes. He visited Bridgewater-Raritan High School last year when he spoke about the accomplishments of Verrazzano. According to Italian Teachers Ms. Bologno and Ms. Di Martino, Mr. Cortigiani provided an in-depth exploration of the historical background of "Calcio Storico" in Florence and his lecture served multiple educational purposes. Ms. Bologno and Ms. DiMartino further detailed his lecture.

title: A lesson in Italian history is shared with Bridgewater-Raritan High School students  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: und of "Calcio Storico" in Florence and his lecture served multiple educational purposes. Ms. Bologno and Ms. DiMartino further detailed his lecture. In the AP Italian and Culture Class, Mr. Cortigiani offered a comprehensive historical explanation of this game, with a detailed explanation of the Middle Ages and Renaissance cultural relevance. In the 4 Honor, the lecture was adapted to meet the needs of this high-achieving group. For Level 2 and Level 3 students, he provided appropriate scaffolding techniques to ensure the accessibility of the content for beginning Italian language learners. The lecture traced the evolution of Calcio Storico Fiorentino from its origins in the Middle Ages to its present-day manifestation. Key points included: The significance of March 25 as Florence's historical New Year's Day and the Feast of the Annunciation (Ascensione). Comparison of Calcio Storico rules with those of other sports, highlighting its unique characteristics. Examination of modern-day celebrations, including color guards, musicians, tambourine performances, and the traditional "crollo del carro" (explosion of the cart). “The visit by Mr.

title: A lesson in Italian history is shared with Bridgewater-Raritan High School students  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ations, including color guards, musicians, tambourine performances, and the traditional "crollo del carro" (explosion of the cart). “The visit by Mr. Marco Cortigiani underscores Bridgewater-Raritan High School's commitment to providing enriching, multicultural educational experiences, enhancing language acquisition and fostering a deeper understanding of global cultures and traditions,’ Ms. Bologno and Ms. DiMartino stated. They added: “The Italian department, along with the entire school community, extends its gratitude to Mr. Cortigiani for his invaluable contribution to our students' education. We look forward to future opportunities for cross-disciplinary learning and cultural exchange.”

The Band Concert was performed on Thursday, March 27, when the third and final musical production of the Festival of the Arts, the showcase event of the highly successful Visual and Performing Arts program in the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District, was presented. Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s Vaughn Stapleton Gym was on the scene for the third time when spectators jammed into the spacious arena to enjoy the final leg of the musical production. The Choral Concert kicked off the Festival of the Arts on March 19, followed by the Orchestra Concert on March 26, and then band students from the intermediate schools, middle school, and high school played on the evening of March 27.

title: High school sophomore artist Lucia Bolle honored with two National Medals  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content High school sophomore artist Lucia Bolle honored with two National Medals Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District March 27, 2025 Lucia Bolle, a sophomore at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, continues to collect awards for her skills as an artist. On March 26, Sarah Nassan, Bridgewater-Raritan High School Art Teacher and Advisor to the National Art Honor Society received notice that Lucia was the recipient of two National Medals in the 2025 Scholastic Art & Writing Awards for her work called Hello, World, which received a Gold Medal, and her work titled Sorrow - The Weight Of Water, received a Gold medal as well as the New York Life award, giving her a $2,500 scholarship. According to Christopher Wisniewski, the Executive Director of the Alliance for Young Artists & Writers, “nearly 110,000 teens entered more than 310,000 original works of art and writing to the 2025 Scholastic Art & Writing Awards. Lucia’s work was selected by some of the foremost leaders in the visual and literary arts for excellence in originality, skill, and the emergence of a personal voice or vision.” He also listed the past winners who went on to claim fame as artists.

title: High school sophomore artist Lucia Bolle honored with two National Medals  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: llence in originality, skill, and the emergence of a personal voice or vision.” He also listed the past winners who went on to claim fame as artists. “Since 1923, the Awards have celebrated teen artists and writers from across the country. You are now part of that legacy, joining the ranks of notable alumni including Tschabalala Self, Stephen King, Kay WalkingStick, Charles White, Joyce Carol Oates, Amanda Gorman, and Andy Warhol—all of whom won Scholastic Awards when they were teens,” Mr. Wisniewski said. “We are proud to publish your work in our online galleries at artandwriting.org . Your name will also be listed in Yearbook 2025, a companion to our annual art and writing anthologies that feature selections of works by National Medalists,” Mr. Wisniewski said. Lucia received the prestigious honor of High Merit for her piece titled "The Show,” during the awards ceremony that was held in the Vaughn Eames Gallery at Kean University on the campus in Union this winter. According to the AANJ, High Merit award winners qualify to move on to nominations to receive the elite New Jersey Governor's Award this spring.

title: Primary Schools can make the call to the bullpen and summon Bridgewater-Raritan High's Operation Elementary  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Primary Schools can make the call to the bullpen and summon Bridgewater-Raritan High's Operation Elementary Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District March 26, 2025 A group of Bridgewater-Raritan High School students are involved in an operation that is quite elementary. Operation Elementary is the name of this well-natured club at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. Their purpose is simple: provide help where and when needed at various functions and events during the year by the seven primary schools in the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District. This robust club offers the workforce—there are 64 active members—to lend a hand to such groups as the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District’s Parent Teacher Organization (PTO), which is busy throughout the school year by sponsoring events. Michele Kussmaul, a Bridgewater-Raritan High School staff member who herself keeps busy, serves as the Advisor to Operation Elementary with Eileen Boosman. Ms. Kussmaul, a Physical Education Teacher and the head coach of the boys’ and girls’ bowling team at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, explained the mission of Operation Elementary.

title: Primary Schools can make the call to the bullpen and summon Bridgewater-Raritan High's Operation Elementary  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: on Teacher and the head coach of the boys’ and girls’ bowling team at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, explained the mission of Operation Elementary. “Operation Elementary is partnering with elementary schools in the Bridgewater-Raritan School District to enhance the childhood experience of our younger students,” Ms. Kussmaul said. She added: “Mentorship, Nostalgia, Community, Connection.” The idea behind creating this productive and helpful group was the brainstorm of high school senior Suraj Shah, a club officer today. “Suraj approached me in October 2024 to start this club. I was his Health and PE teacher,” Ms. Kussmaul recalled. Thanks to his initiative, Operation Elementary is ready to come out of the bullpen at any time. Just this past weekend, on March 22, Operation Elementary was there to help with the clothing drive held at the John F. Kennedy Primary School. The club will also assist at the high school’s Vaughn Stapleton Gymnasium on Saturday, March 29, when the Council of School Associations sponsors its Scoring Points for Kids basketball fundraiser.

title: Primary Schools can make the call to the bullpen and summon Bridgewater-Raritan High's Operation Elementary  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Vaughn Stapleton Gymnasium on Saturday, March 29, when the Council of School Associations sponsors its Scoring Points for Kids basketball fundraiser. Operation Elementary has invited primary school students and their families to the fun evening that will start at 6 p.m. and run until 8:30 p.m. in the Bridgewater-Raritan High School auditorium. Educational research is abundantly clear: possession of cell phones within instructional environments negatively impacts learning. Not only does cell phone use negatively impact one’s ability to retain information, but it also specifically correlates with lower test grades. Moreover, research suggests that cell phone use by other students within the classroom, will negatively affect learning for every student in the classroom, even those not specifically using cell phones. As a result, within instructional environments throughout BRHS, cell phones are not permitted to be present.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Mia Hassouna received  prestigious theatre award from Montclair State University  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Mia Hassouna received prestigious theatre award from Montclair State University Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District June 4, 2025 The tireless efforts produced off stage can be as valuable as the acting on stage. That role of the crucial work behind the curtain was recognized when Mia Hassouna, a junior at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, was honored at the 2024-2025 Montclair State University’s Theatre Foxy Awards on Monday, May 19. Mia received the Outstanding Achievement By A Stage Manager For A Play Award. She was nominated for Montclair State University’s Theatre Foxy Awards with fellow Bridgewater-Raritan High School Theatre Arts Company students who participated on the cast and crew for two 2024 productions—Radium Girls in December 2024, and High School Musical in May 2024. Heather Diaforli-Day, the Bridgewater-Raritan High School Theatre Arts Director, strongly believed that Mia was a worthy choice to receive this elite award. “Everyone in the Bridgewater-Raritan Theatre Arts program knows that this is a very well-deserved award,” Ms. Diaforli-Day said.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Mia Hassouna received  prestigious theatre award from Montclair State University  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ve this elite award. “Everyone in the Bridgewater-Raritan Theatre Arts program knows that this is a very well-deserved award,” Ms. Diaforli-Day said. “Mia is our beloved stage manager and works incredibly hard to keep the wheels turning during rehearsals in backstage during productions. I am thrilled that she has been recognized for all that she does.” Mia will be performing her award-winning tasks on Friday, June 6, when when the June Theatre Showcase is presented in the high school auditorium at 7 p.m.

title: Nine Bridgewater-Raritan High School students attended physics conference at Drew University | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Nine Bridgewater-Raritan High School students attended physics conference at Drew University Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District June 3, 2025 Nine students from Bridgewater-Raritan High School spent a day at Drew University, participating in a Student Masterclass on Elementary Particle Physics organized by the Drew QuarkNet Center on April 5. This student masterclass on elementary particle physics was a fantastic opportunity for physics students to work on real particle accelerator data from CERN, Geneva, Switzerland. They were able to test the energy, mass, and momentum conservation principles we have worked on in class, using experimental data from the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) and the Compact Muon Solenoid (CMS) at CERN in Switzerland. The students were Piya Advani, Arjun Agarwal, Alice Jiang, Alexander Bruno, Colin Hawley, Mohit Srinivasan, Nandika Nambiar, Mayur Kashyap, and Tianyi Li. Dr. Eleni Arapaki, the high school’s AP Physics 1 & 2 Teacher, explained the day in the following summary. The LHC accelerates protons to almost 7500 times the energy equivalent of their mass.

title: Nine Bridgewater-Raritan High School students attended physics conference at Drew University | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: sics 1 & 2 Teacher, explained the day in the following summary. The LHC accelerates protons to almost 7500 times the energy equivalent of their mass. The protons circulate in opposite directions and collide in the center of CMS. But protons are not just particles: they are more like bags of quarks and gluons. When protons collide, all sorts of very short-lived particles can be made from all that energy. At the most simplistic level, electrons are responsible for the electricity that lights our houses and turns on our computers. Muons are some sort of heavier cousin of the electron, but we're not sure just what the relationship is. This Mu2e experiment will help us understand that relationship, and so understanding muons is part of understanding the electrons that power our society. During the masterclass, pairs of students examined sets of 100 actual events from the CMS WZH experiment at CERN using iSpy-webgl, an online three-dimensional event display. Events are characterized as 1-, 2-, or 4-lepton (excluding any neutrinos) and used the event display to find invariant masses of possible parent particles of the 2- and 4-lepton events.

title: Nine Bridgewater-Raritan High School students attended physics conference at Drew University | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: or 4-lepton (excluding any neutrinos) and used the event display to find invariant masses of possible parent particles of the 2- and 4-lepton events. Students entered results in the online CMS Instrument for Masterclass Analysis (CIMA), which creates 2- and 4-lepton mass plots and counts electrons, muons, and W+ and W- candidates, so that students may derive simple but important ratios that characterize the data. Supported by QuarkNet, Fermilab, and international partners, masterclasses enable students to be particle physicists for a day. The students had the chance to learn about the Standard Model and experimental particle physics, meet with panelists from Drew and Notre Dame Universities, attend presentations on AI implementation on Big Data Analysis, work together to analyze authentic data and participate in a video conference with scientists from Fermi Lab, the University of Mexico, and two schools, one from Mexico and one from Colombia. . “The students who have participated in this masterclass may make a report and publish their results. Some of our students took advantage of this opportunity and we cannot wait to see their work published,” Dr. Arapaki said.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School will present its June Theatre Showcase on June 6  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School will present its June Theatre Showcase on June 6 Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District June 1, 2025 Wearing two hats is something that Paige D’Aloisio is accustomed to. Enjoying two different roles is something that this sophomore at Bridgewater-Raritan High School enjoys, too. Paige is working as Assistant Stage Manager while the high school’s Thespian Honor Society Troupe No. 5154 and the Theatre Company are presenting two one-act plays during the June Theatre Showcase on Friday, June 6. The high school auditorium will serve as the venue at 7 p.m. when the Thespian Honor Society produces Little Women , and then the Theatre Company performs Brothers Grimm Spectaculathon. Paige is serving in her role off stage after she had been on stage in early April when she acted in the high school’s Spring Musical, Footloose. “I like it and it is good to work and have a hands-on experience,” Paige shared about her position as Assistant Stage Manager during rehearsal on the afternoon of Friday, May 30. “But I also like being in the show when I played in Footloose .

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School will present its June Theatre Showcase on June 6  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ition as Assistant Stage Manager during rehearsal on the afternoon of Friday, May 30. “But I also like being in the show when I played in Footloose . I like the different views from both jobs.” She and her colleagues, who are on stage and off, are coordinating the entire production. All under the watchful eye of Heather Diaforli-Day, the high school’s dedicated Director of Theatre Arts. She explained the setup for the June Theatre Showcase. “The students have been responsible for casting, directing, costumes, props, lighting, sets, sound, and publicity. I have witnessed an incredible amount of growth in all of the students involved, as they learn how to carry out each piece of the production,” Ms. Diaforli-Day said. The Thespian Honor Society’s Little Women by Marisha Chamberlain is a play about the four young March sisters, under their guidance of their mother, struggle to keep their family going while their father is away in the Civil War. The Theatre Company’s Brothers Grimm Spectaculathon by Dan Zolidis will feature two narrators who will attempt to recreate all 209 of the fairy tales of the Brothers Grimm in a wild, ast-paced extravaganza.

title: A ceremony was held when Bridgewater-Raritan High School students were inducted into the National Honor Society  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content A ceremony was held when Bridgewater-Raritan High School students were inducted into the National Honor Society Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 30, 2025 By Paritosh Bhole The Prowler , Co-Editor-In-Chief The Bridgewater-Raritan High School chapter of the National Honor Society (NHS) held its 2025 induction ceremony in the auditorium on May 28. Students accepted into the society, along with friends and family members, were invited to a special ceremony where they would be recognized and introduced to the society and its core values. The National Honor Society predicates its work on four main pillars of traits: scholarship, leadership, service and character. The Bridgewater-Raritan chapter, led by advisor . Denise Cimpko-Beller, has been active for several years, setting up volunteer programs at various important school events such as the annual freshman orientations. Junior students are eligible to be invited to the honor society, becoming fully active members in their senior year.

title: A ceremony was held when Bridgewater-Raritan High School students were inducted into the National Honor Society  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: he annual freshman orientations. Junior students are eligible to be invited to the honor society, becoming fully active members in their senior year. For the new members, the process began when they were invited to apply to the National Honor Society, fitting the eligibility criteria, which included a minimum grade point average and a positive standing in classes. Students were then tasked with filling out the entire National Honor Society application, which included multiple references that acted as testaments to the character and integrity of the students. After all the applications were submitted and received, a special advisory committee from the honor society chapter reviewed all the candidates and selected individuals who would be inducted into the organization. Accepted candidates were contacted and invited to the induction ceremony. Starting promptly at 6:15 p.m., the ceremony began with NHS President Sharanya Kothare providing opening remarks, welcoming the crowd of family members and friends to the special event. Accepted students sat on chairs on the auditorium stage as the society’s other leadership seniors explained each of the society’s aforementioned core values.

title: A ceremony was held when Bridgewater-Raritan High School students were inducted into the National Honor Society  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: students sat on chairs on the auditorium stage as the society’s other leadership seniors explained each of the society’s aforementioned core values. Once the remarks had concluded, the spotlight was directed towards the students as each accepted member was called up to accept their certificate in alphabetical order. Audience members cheered as each student walked across the stage, a moment of pride and triumph in their academic career. After all of the students received their certificates, final remarks were provided, highlighted by words of encouragement and advice from Principal Daniel Hemberger. Once all the speeches had concluded, students and audience members were dismissed to take photos, socialize with one another and purchase goods and merchandise for the school’s NHS chapter. Junior Ronak Hunashimarad reflected on his acceptance into the honor society. “NHS was the culmination of years of academic excellence and service to the community. It is nice to be recognized for our efforts,” he said.

title: A ceremony was held when Bridgewater-Raritan High School students were inducted into the National Honor Society  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ociety. “NHS was the culmination of years of academic excellence and service to the community. It is nice to be recognized for our efforts,” he said. Acceptance into the National Honor Society is one of the most remarkable achievements for students, and the volume of new inductees at Bridgewater-Raritan High School encapsulates the success and abilities of the high school’s students. NHS Co-Vice President and senior Siyona Naidu spoke about what it means to be a part of the NHS chapter. “NHS is a great outlet to volunteer and make a difference in the Bridgewater-Raritan community. I really enjoy getting to organize events, and I like seeing the positive impact our seniors have, ” she said. title: A week to remember was experienced by the Bridgewater-Raritan High School choir students | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content A week to remember was experienced by the Bridgewater-Raritan High School choir students Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 30, 2025 It was quite a memorable—and eventful— week for the choir students at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. According to Lisa Rotondi, Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s dedicated and enthusiastic Director of Choirs, the Bridgewater-Raritan High School choir students first received awards and will then attend a festival at Six Flags Great Adventure. And, yes, they also found the time on Tuesday, May 27, to successfully perform during their “A Spring Choral Concert.” The high school auditorium was the setting when the six choir groups produced a spectacular concert to the delight of an attentive audience. The six Bridgewater-Raritan High School choir groups who performed were the Glee Ensemble, the Select Treble Choir, the Mixed Choir, the Lorelei Ensemble, the Choir Seniors and the Symphonic Choir. The final set was then sung by the Combined Choirs. Ms. Rotondi shared her passion of her choir students. “I was so proud of the choirs after their performance on Tuesday evening.

We have grown together over the past school year in not only our musical skills but also interpersonally as well. I know that many of these singers will remain friends for life," she said. Ms.Rotondi outlined what was a busy and productive week. “A highlight of our performance season was last week when we attended the NJ ACDA High School Festival and received Superior ratings in all choirs, plus an Exemplary (perfect) score in Sight-Reading for Symphonic Choir,’ Ms. Rotondi reported. “Our run of spring performances will conclude this weekend (May 30-June 1) at the Music in Parks festival at Six Flags Great Adventure. One audience member who loudly applauded the concert showing was Matt McCarthy, the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District Supervisor of Arts Education. “The evening’s program provided an excellent presentation of what our choral offerings aim to achieve.
