title: A ceremony to honor Governor's Award-winning artist Lucia Bolle was performed at Bridgewater-Raritan High School  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content A ceremony to honor Governor's Award-winning artist Lucia Bolle was performed at Bridgewater-Raritan High School Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 29, 2025 Perhaps the finest scholastic artist in New Jersey once again resides at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. And this is no waggish notion. Lucia Bolle, a sophomore at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, was honored during a ceremony on Thursday afternoon, May 29, when she received the 2025 Art Administrators of New Jersey (AANJ) Visual Art Student Achievement Award as well as the Governor’s Award in Arts Education. This elite ceremony was performed in the Bridgewater-Raritan High School auditorium, where New Jersey State and school district officials, as well as family members and high school administrators, staff and students were in attendance. Lucia’s fellow Art students were excused from class, and they assembled in the auditorium seats to support their fellow schoolmate. High School Principal Dan Hemberger opened the festivities with a welcome, while Matt McCarthy, the Supervisor of Arts Education for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District, introduced the guests.

title: A ceremony to honor Governor's Award-winning artist Lucia Bolle was performed at Bridgewater-Raritan High School  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: es with a welcome, while Matt McCarthy, the Supervisor of Arts Education for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District, introduced the guests. On hand to present a medallion, a check worth $250, a certificate, and a proclamation from New Jersey Gov. Murphy were Margaret El, the Executive Board Member of the AANJ, Alisha Sheth of the Arts Ed NJ Program and Outreach Coordinator, and Shea Murray, the Visual and Performing Arts Coordinator for the New Jersey Department of Education. Joining Principal Hemberger and Mr. McCarthy were Superintendent Bob Beers, Assistant Superintendent for Personnel Dr. Dan Fonder, Board of Education President Steve Singer, Board of Education member Barry Walker, Jason Mauriello, the Director of 21st Century Learning and Data Analysis for the school district, Assistant High School Principal Damian Storey, and Sarah Nasson, Lucia’s Art Teacher. The audience also included one proud mother, Ana Bolle. Lucia’s prestigious honor completed a hat trick for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District.

title: A ceremony to honor Governor's Award-winning artist Lucia Bolle was performed at Bridgewater-Raritan High School  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ence also included one proud mother, Ana Bolle. Lucia’s prestigious honor completed a hat trick for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District. Before Lucia earned this achievement for 2025, Crystal Zhang received the Visual Art Student Achievement Award in 2023, and Sharanya Ram was the recipient in 2024. Each of those two students was a senior at Bridgewater-Raritan High School when honored. Lucia became a candidate to become the third straight Bridgewater-Raritan High School artist to win this elite award when her work titled “The Show” was selected as a finalist for the Governor’s Award when the AANJ sponsored its 36th Annual Emerging Artists Art Show at the James Howe Gallery on Kean University’s campus on Sunday, January 28. It was during that exhibit at the college in Union when Ms. El realized that she had seen a winner. “When we (AANJ) hung up Lucia's artwork in the gallery, everyone said ‘look at this,' '' Ms. El recalled. That recognition placed Lucia in contention with an estimated “35 to 50 other students,” who were in contention for the Governor’s Award, according to Ms. El. The finalists, including Lucia, then submitted a portfolio of their pieces.

title: A ceremony to honor Governor's Award-winning artist Lucia Bolle was performed at Bridgewater-Raritan High School  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: s,” who were in contention for the Governor’s Award, according to Ms. El. The finalists, including Lucia, then submitted a portfolio of their pieces. That portfolio sealed the deal for Lucia. “Her technique and the way she used the lighting,” Ms. El said about the skills that boosted Lucia to the top of an impressive list of student artists. Mr. McCarthy spoke highly of her work ethic and determination. “When you look at Lucia's work, you may first notice her technical skill—and it is considerable. You may be drawn to the beauty she creates or the power of her images. But I ask you instead to recognize the courage in her work,” he stated. “The courage of someone who has learned to trust her vision, who has developed the discipline to translate that vision into reality, and who has found the strength to share her most authentic self through her art. This is what makes Lucia not just a skilled artist but an inspiring one.” Mr. McCarthy concluded: “I am honored to have Lucia as part of our arts community here in Bridgewater-Raritan. Not because of this recognition today, significant as it is, but because of her ongoing bravery to create.” Ms.

Lucia, who revealed that she became seriously involved in an artwork when she was “about 12 or 13,” thanked the audience for attending. “I am appreciative and always grateful that we have a fantastic art community,” she said. Her mom, Ana Bolle, was asked how proud she was after the ceremony. “On a scale of 1 to 10, I would say a 20.” Below is Lucia’s winning portfolio that was submitted to the AANJ.

title: BRRSD Reviews 2024-25 Goals, Citing Gains in Student Achievement and Equity | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content BRRSD Reviews 2024-25 Goals, Citing Gains in Student Achievement and Equity Bridgewater Raritan Regional School District May 27, 2025 Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District (BRRSD) provided updates to its 2024-25 district goals, emphasizing student achievement, equitable outcomes for all students, and enhanced community engagement. On Tuesday, May 13, during the BRRSD Board of Education meeting, Robert Beers, Superintendent of Schools, reported notable academic progress, highlighted by a 97% graduation rate—up from 93% in 2022 and substantially above the New Jersey State average of 91%. The percentage of high school students enrolled in Advanced Placement (AP) courses rose to 50.5%, with a projected increase to 57.6% next year. BRRSD has also expanded its dual enrollment program from 4 to 17 college-level courses and increased student participation from 40 to 133, partnering with Rutgers, Seton Hall, and The College of New Jersey. Students earning the Seal of Biliteracy awards more than doubled, from 80 in 2023-24 to 172 in 2024-25, recognizing proficiency in 20 languages.

title: BRRSD Reviews 2024-25 Goals, Citing Gains in Student Achievement and Equity | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: sey. Students earning the Seal of Biliteracy awards more than doubled, from 80 in 2023-24 to 172 in 2024-25, recognizing proficiency in 20 languages. Equity and inclusion remain central, with early implementation of full-day kindergarten set for 2025, one year ahead of schedule. Gifted and talented identification in grades K–4 increased from 351 to 407 students, and tiered intervention needs in grades K–6 decreased from 21% to 13%. The district is also enhancing mental health and wellness initiatives, including suicide prevention training, a 9th-grade transition program, and a structured learning lab that demonstrated improvement in 64% of participating students. Major facilities investments include secure vestibules, HVAC upgrades, and a 6th-grade addition to the Middle School. With expanded communication tools, sustainable budget practices, and continued collaboration with local organizations, BRRSD remains committed to fostering excellence and opportunity for all learners.

title: Artworks by Bridgewater-Raritan High School students displayed at the BRRSD Visual Arts Show  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Artworks by Bridgewater-Raritan High School students displayed at the BRRSD Visual Arts Show Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 27, 2025 The gallery was jam-packed with skillful and thoughtful artworks. The gallery was also jam-packed with attentive and curious visitors. The fourth and final leg of the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District’s 2025 Festival of the Arts was staged on Thursday, May 22, with the presentation of the Visual Arts Show. Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s main cafeteria and small gym were transformed into art galleries that attracted an estimated crowd of 1,500 throughout the evening. A spectacular gallery in the small gymnasium, as well as the outside hallways, featured the talents of many high school artists.. The cafeteria’s exhibit displayed the crafts done by more high school students, all the way down to the primary school artists. All 11 schools in the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District were represented. A spectacular collection of ceramic works was also on display. Matt McCarthy, the District Supervisor for Arts Education, was among the crowd that evening.

title: Artworks by Bridgewater-Raritan High School students displayed at the BRRSD Visual Arts Show  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School

Matt McCarthy, the District Supervisor for Arts Education, was among the crowd that evening. “Our District visual arts staff put together a wonderful collection of student work,” Mr. McCarthy revealed. “So much time goes into preparing an exhibit like this. I am so grateful for the dedication and passion they bring to our District.” Mr. McCarthy further marveled at the collection of fine artworks. “The visual arts offer a unique format for exploring ideas. It is all about asking questions, experimentation, and the development of personal artistic voice,” he said. “The hard work students have put into developing these skills was on full display.” The 2025 Festival of the Arts presented the Chorus Concert, the Orchestra Concert, and the Band Concert during the month of March at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School orchestra students bid farewell with a memorable performance at the Spring Concert  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School orchestra students bid farewell with a memorable performance at the Spring Concert Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 22, 2025 Their last tango in Bridgewater-Raritan highlighted the last orchestra concert of 2025. Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s Spring Orchestra Concert, as expected, provided the splendid sounds of the gifted and talented student musicians who performed under their passionate and energetic director on the evening of Tuesday, May 20, in the high school auditorium. But what was not expected was when the enthusiastic crowd was treated to a farewell tango dance by two graduating senior orchestra students, as well as a superb solo violin presentation. During the final number played by the Senior Orchestra, senior members Tia Sharma and Brandon Wu rose from their chairs on stage halfway through the performance and danced the tango for the remainder of the piece. This energetic dancing routine, which drew a standing ovation at the conclusion, was a repeat from when these two students first performed the dance at the concert when they were high school freshmen in 2022.

The Bridgewater-Raritan High School Orchestra Director, Hsiao-yu Li (Sherry) Griggs, recalled their first dance and will forever recall their last dance. “They treated the audience with a very special tango that they performed four years ago. However, this time, they added even more flair and passion for this farewell performance,’ Ms. Griggs said. She also singled out the single performance of Navin Agarwal, the first chair violinist, who delivered an improv with a solo violin part. “They are such a talented group of students,” Ms. Girggs said about those three student musicians. The groups who performed through the evening were the Ninth Orchestra, Piu Vivace, the Concert Orchestra, the Symphony Orchestra, and the Senior Orchestra. “The Bridgewater-Raritan High School Orchestra Spring Concerts are always such a bittersweet event for me,” Ms. Griggs claimed. “This year is no exception.” She praised the growth in their musical skills. “I am extremely proud of the students' improvement in their craft and music literacy.

content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School DECA students received awards at prestigious national conference in Florida Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 22, 2025 Bridgewater-Raritan High School students who are members of DECA were recognized with elite awards while enjoying a visit to Florida. According to Shilpa Wakde, the Bridgewater-Raritan High School DECA Advisor, reported on “the outstanding accomplishments of our DECA students at the 2025 International Career Development Conference (ICDC), held from April 26–29 in Orlando, Florida. Ms. Wakde explained that this prestigious event brought together over 20,000 emerging leaders from around the globe to compete, learn, and connect in the fields of marketing, finance, hospitality, and management. title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School's EPIC Yearbook honored again with elite award  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School's EPIC Yearbook honored again with elite award Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 21, 2025 The exceptional production of The EPIC, Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s yearbook, has been recognized once again. The highly popular yearbook received the honor of Award of Excellence from the Walsworth Gallery of Excellence. The EPIC received this same award in 2023. Mike Bittner, the EPIC Yearbook Editorial Co-Advisor with Alice Kurian-Haldey and also is the EPIC Yearbook Business Advisor, was thrilled to share the fantastic news. “Our yearbook staff has been awarded this prestigious Award of Excellence from our publisher,’ Mr. Bittner said. “This is a great achievement because it means that our yearbook will be shared with other schools throughout the nation as an example of how to create and design a yearbook.” Mr. Bitnner also reported that there is not too much time remaining to purchase this award-winning yearbook. “This year's edition of the yearbook is now 80 percent sold, and we only have 275 copies available for purchase. This can be done at www.yearbookforever.com ,” he stated.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School Robotics Team 303 gearing up for STEAM Week | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School Robotics Team 303 gearing up for STEAM Week Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 19, 2025 Spreading the word is the mission of the students who are members of the Robotics Team 303 at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. And that word is actually five words—Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics. When you add the words up, it spells STEAM. In an effort to “spread” what STEAM is all about in schools, Team 303 will sponsor its annual–and highly successful—STEAM Week Program. The program will run each day from Monday, June 2, through Friday, June 6, from 5 p.m. to 7 p.m. Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s two cafeterias will be the site of the exhibits that are prepared to educate and entertain students from Grades 2 through 7 in the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District. According to Team 303, students will be grouped by grade level and will engage in fun, team-building activities. The day will also feature a routine of having an ice-breaker activity, a keynote presentation, three hands-on activities, and a collaborative game to conclude the day.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School Robotics Team 303 gearing up for STEAM Week | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: feature a routine of having an ice-breaker activity, a keynote presentation, three hands-on activities, and a collaborative game to conclude the day. Team 303’s ringleader for this educational week is Rohit Mudduluru. The high school junior explained the club’s mission for STEAM Week. “We want to spread STEAM throughout the community,” Rohit revealed. “We want the kids to have fun while doing STEAM.” He and his colleagues will offer lessons that youngerts can pick up besides their usual lessons in the classroom. “STEAM could be hard for kids to do in school, so we offer them a chance to learn outside of class, and we will have 15 to 20 mentors each day working with them,” Rohit explained. “A lot of people can't do STEAM by themselves.” The mentors during the week will be members of Team 303. John Sirusas, the dedicated Advisor of Team 303, is proud of his students for this event that can serve as a recruiting tool for the club. “This is a great introduction for younger kids and we hope that they can join our club when they enter high school,” said Mr. Sirusas, who added that the STEAM Week has been functioning for about 10 years.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School Robotics Team 303 gearing up for STEAM Week | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: pe that they can join our club when they enter high school,” said Mr. Sirusas, who added that the STEAM Week has been functioning for about 10 years. Sophia Mendez, a sophomore Team 303 member who is one of the mainstays of the STEAM Week, shared her thoughts about the event. “We want to spread the ideals of STEAM to the kids and inspire them so that they may want to have a career in these fields,’ she said. Alex Masin, a high school junior, and Arshitha Harikrishna, a high school sophomore, are two other Team 303 members who will be key contributors to STEAM Week. “This is a free way to have fun and learn STEAM,” Rohit concluded. title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School first-year physics students thrived in the PhysicsBowl Contest  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School first-year physics students thrived in the PhysicsBowl Contest Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 16, 2025 A team of 15 students from Bridgewater-Raritan High School made quite an impression in their first year as physics students. So much of an impression that they finished second in New Jersey when Bridgewater-Raritan High School sponsored these 15 students to participate in the 2025 PhysicsBowl Contest. The PhysicsBowl Contest is an international high school competition organized by the American Association of Physics Teachers (AAPT). According to the AAPT, school teams compete regionally with other school teams by taking a 40-question, 45-minute, timed, multiple-choice test under their school’s supervision. Exam questions are based on topics and concepts covered in a typical high school physics course. According to Dr. Eleni Arapaki, the AP Physics 1 and 2 Teacher at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, the exam was presented on March 28 at the high school. The team consisted of 15 students from AP Physics classes under Dr. Arapaki, Paul Wargo and Fangze Shang.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School first-year physics students thrived in the PhysicsBowl Contest  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: s presented on March 28 at the high school. The team consisted of 15 students from AP Physics classes under Dr. Arapaki, Paul Wargo and Fangze Shang. Bridgewater-Raritan delivered a second-place finish in Division 1 for Region 4, which consisted of schools throughout New Jersey Bridgewater-Raritan was the runner-up to the Peddie School, while Pioneer Academy of Wayne was third and Northern Valley Regional High at Old Tappan finished in fourth place. The AAPT developed Division 1 for first-year physics students. Team scores for the school are determined by the sum of the scores of the top five students competing. “Each Division has 15 regions that have been established across the country to allow schools in each region to compete against one another. Specialized math and science schools compete in their own region,” Dr. Arapaki said The Bridgewater-Raritan High School students received certificates for their outstanding performance in the Physics Bowl.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School student Alicia McCann honored with elite award  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School student Alicia McCann honored with elite award Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 15, 2025 Alicia McCann is proof of what the Rebecca S. McDonald Award is all about. The student at Bridgewater-Raritan High School was the recipient of the prestigious Rebecca S. McDonald Award during a ceremony that was held on Wednesday, May 14, at Bell Works in Holmdel. She was joined at the ceremony by her parents as well as Dr. Sandra Lynch, who is the Transition Coordinator for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District, and her teacher, Brenda Hockstein, the School to Employment Program Coordinator at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. Alicia was one of six high school seniors who were chosen by the New Jersey Transition Coordinator’s Network to receive the Rebecca S. McDonald Award. According to the Association of People Supporting Employment First, “Rebecca McDonald was a tireless advocate and champion for supported employment. This award recognizes students involved in successful transition activities, preparing them for the adult world.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School student Alicia McCann honored with elite award  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: d champion for supported employment. This award recognizes students involved in successful transition activities, preparing them for the adult world. Students are nominated based on the following criteria: Overcoming obstacles, motivation, and achievement in the areas of transition from school to adult life. Areas of transition considered: Self-advocacy, postsecondary planning, paid or unpaid employment experiences, participation in their IEP development, independent living, or any other area applicable to the student’s transition.” During the ceremony at Bell Works, Alicia received a $150 gift card and a framed certificate. Below is a heartwarming and inspiring message that Dr. Lynch, who nominated Alicia for the Rebecca S. McDonald Award, wrote about Alicia. “Alicia is an inspiring young adult who brings her absolute best to every opportunity, whether volunteering at a business in her community or engaging with new topics in her pilot college-based program. Alicia exhibits incredible leadership, curiosity, dedication, and enthusiasm for learning. She is internally proud and motivated and participates in all aspects of her education.

Her exuberant attitude and perseverance make her a wonderful role model and candidate for this award. In my 10-plus years as a member of the Transition Coordinator’s Network, I have yet to put a name forward for this prestigious award, but no longer. The growth we have seen in you this year has been immeasurable. Your mind, your heart, and your humor will be missed, but we fear not.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District honored with prestigious music award for eighth straight year | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District honored with prestigious music award for eighth straight year Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 12, 2025 The streak continues for the gifted Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District Music Department. For the eighth consecutive year, the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District Music Department was the recipient of the prestigious Best Communities for Music Education award from the National Association of Music Merchants (NAMM). According to the NAMM, the award program recognizes and celebrates outstanding efforts by teachers, administrators, parents, students, and community leaders who have made music education part of a well-rounded education. The NAMM announced that 935 school districts are among the Best Communities in the nation for music education for the year 2025. According to the NAMM, the award program recognizes and celebrates outstanding efforts by teachers, administrators, parents, students, and community leaders who have made music education part of a well-rounded education.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District honored with prestigious music award for eighth straight year | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: tanding efforts by teachers, administrators, parents, students, and community leaders who have made music education part of a well-rounded education. Designations are made to districts and schools that demonstrate an exceptionally high commitment to and access to music education. Matt McCarthy, who is the Supervisor for Arts Education for the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District, was thrilled when he was contacted by the NAMM with the news on Monday, May 12. “I am incredibly proud to have once again had Bridgewater-Raritan named a Best Community for Music Education by the NAMM Foundation. As a department, we don’t take this distinction lightly,” Mr. McCarthy stated. “ Our staff of dedicated music educators is deeply committed to providing only the best opportunities for students to grow academically, socially, and intellectually through the study of music.” Mr. McCarthy continued to share his pleasure about the elite music program at the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District.

I invite everyone to attend any of our district concerts and see for themselves.” The community will have that opportunity to accept Mr. McCarthy’s invitation. title: Five Bridgewater-Raritan High School student musicians accepted into prestigious All-Eastern Band and Orchestra | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Five Bridgewater-Raritan High School student musicians accepted into prestigious All-Eastern Band and Orchestra Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 9, 2025 Five Bridgewater-Raritan High School student musicians have earned prestigious honors. These five gifted students were accepted into the All-Eastern Band and Orchestra. Julian DiMaggio (Percussion), Grade 12 James Krempasky (Euphonium), Grade 11 Fisayo Ogunsulire (Trumpet), Grade 11 Ananth Raman (Trumpet), Grade 11 David Yi (Cello), Grade 12 According to Lisa Rottondi, who is the Director of Choirs at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, the achievement of five students being accepted is “actually a huge number from one school.” “These students represent some of the finest high school musicians across 11 northeastern states. The All-Eastern ensembles only take place every other year and only juniors and seniors in high school are eligible to be considered,’ Ms. Rotondi stated. She also revealed the process.

title: Five Bridgewater-Raritan High School student musicians accepted into prestigious All-Eastern Band and Orchestra | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: place every other year and only juniors and seniors in high school are eligible to be considered,’ Ms. Rotondi stated. She also revealed the process. “The highest scoring students from All-State ensembles are selected to participate, using a formula that also accounts for the state's population, allowing for a similar percentage of students to attend from each state.” Ms. Rotondi added. “They traveled to Connecticut from April 23-27 and worked with college professors from across the country before performing a concert at the historic Mortensen Hall at the Bushnell Performing Arts Center”.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School students Anika Seshan, Ruby Sethi and Lauren Yang attended engineering conference at Princeton University  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School students Anika Seshan, Ruby Sethi and Lauren Yang attended engineering conference at Princeton University Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 8, 2025 Bridgewater-Raritan High School’s Anika Seshan, Ruby Sethi, and Lauren Yang, who are AP Physics 1 students, attended the Princeton Spring High School Engineering Colloquium organized by the Princeton University Society of Women Engineers on Saturday, April 19. According to Dr. Eleni Arapaki, who is the AP Physics 1 and 2 Teacher at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, the Princeton chapter of the National Society of Women Engineers (SWE) is an organization that supports women in engineering and also dedicates time to encouraging younger women to become engineers. “One of the organization’s major goals is outreach, and in a rapidly developing world with endless opportunities for discovery, creativity, and invention, building a strong workforce of engineers is essential to moving new ideas forward,” Dr. Arapaki said. Dr. Arapaki continued her report on the all-day session at Princeton.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School students Anika Seshan, Ruby Sethi and Lauren Yang attended engineering conference at Princeton University  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: force of engineers is essential to moving new ideas forward,” Dr. Arapaki said. Dr. Arapaki continued her report on the all-day session at Princeton. “The event series is designed to introduce high school students to the field of engineering and provide them with an excellent opportunity to make connections with current female engineers and other students interested in STEM. The event included a panel of Princeton professors with a Q&A session, a panel of students studying the various fields of engineering with chances for small group discussion, a hands-on engineering activity led by Princeton engineering students, and an expo of various engineering extracurriculars at Princeton, ranging from their car racing club to their aerospace and rocket launching club! “ Dr. Arapaki concluded: “Our students had the opportunity to tour the E-Quad, Princeton's Engineering Department, where they competed in a bridge-building competition and won first place! The experience was really fun and exciting!”

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School's  Parinita Chandrashekar honored as winner of 2025 Congressional Art Competition  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: Skip to content Bridgewater-Raritan High School's Parinita Chandrashekar honored as winner of 2025 Congressional Art Competition Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District May 8, 2025 The United States Capitol Building is the next stop for the artwork of Parinita Chandrashekar, a senior at Bridgewater-Raritan High School. Congressman Tom Kean, Jr. (NJ-07) announced on Sunday, May 4, that Parinita won the 2025 Congressional Art Competition for New Jersey’s Seventh Congressional District. Parinita’s drawing, titled “Cutting Through Reality,” landed her first place in this prestigious contest, and it also landed her artwork in Washington, D.C., where the drawing will be displayed in the U.S. Capitol for one year. She has also been invited to a June ceremony in Washington, where her drawing and the artworks of other winning students from around the country will be unveiled. Congressman Kean announced the winners during a Congressional Art Competition Recognition Event in Flemington. All participating student artists and their families were invited to attend the affair on May 4. Each student was recognized for their creativity and dedication.

title: Bridgewater-Raritan High School's  Parinita Chandrashekar honored as winner of 2025 Congressional Art Competition  | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: ating student artists and their families were invited to attend the affair on May 4. Each student was recognized for their creativity and dedication. Two runners-up were also recognized: Laurie Wu of Ridge High School for “Birth,” and Deeksha Chaganur of Somerset County Vocational and Technical High School for “Pines and Petals.” Their pieces will be exhibited in Congressman Kean’s offices in New Jersey and Washington, D.C., on a rotating basis throughout the year. A panel of professional judges selected all winners based on artistic skill, design, visual appeal, and originality. "Our district is home to many exceptionally talented young artists, and the incredible submissions we received this year were truly impressive," Congressman Tom Kean, Jr. said. "Congratulations to Parinita, whose remarkable artwork will be displayed in the U.S. Capitol, representing the creativity and spirit of our district. I also want to thank all 51 students who participated, along with their art teachers, judges, and families. Their dedication and support are what made this year’s competition such a success." Another Bridgewater-Raritan High School student was also recognized during the ceremony.

As part of her ongoing effort to make French more immersive and meaningful, she invited two special guest speakers to visit during unit lunch and remain for her AP French class.
Her cousin Anna, a college graduate with a degree in English, shared her experience teaching English in France through the TAPIF (Teaching Assistant Program in France) initiative. During her year abroad, Anna lived and worked in a small French town, where she fully embraced the culture, language, and lifestyle.

title: Live Feed | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content:
nce) initiative. During her year abroad, Anna lived and worked in a small French town, where she fully embraced the culture, language, and lifestyle. Her story gave students a firsthand look at what it’s like to live and work in France after college.
Joining Anna was her friend Lorène, a native of France currently studying law with the goal of becoming a judge. Lorène brought an authentic French perspective to the discussion, speaking about her education, her career goals, and what life is like for young adults in France today.
French 4A and French 4H students gathered during unit lunch to hear both Anna and Lorène speak about their experiences. Afterward, Ms. Kostis’ AP French students had the special opportunity to participate in a Q&A session entirely in French, challenging their language skills and giving them the chance to hold real conversations with native and fluent speakers.
These kinds of events have been part of Ms. Kostis’ efforts all year long to enrich students’ understanding of the French language and culture.

title: Live Feed | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content:
ivered a spectacular performance during their “A Spring Choral Concert” that was presented on Tuesday evening, May 27, in the high school auditorium. Lisa Rotondi, the Bridgewater-Raritan High School Choir Director, was joined by Christopher Fortin on piano when seven total groups sang to the delight of the audience.
The choirs were the Glee Ensemble, the Select Treble Choir, the Mixed Choir, the Lorelei Ensemble, the Choir Seniors, the Symphonic Choir, and then the concert was capped by the singing of the Combined Choirs. Here are some scenes from the concert. 10 months ago, Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District Each year, Lockheed Martin, an aerospace and defense company, is the host to Code Quest, an annual international computer programming competition, taking place in person at Lockheed Martin facilities and virtually. According to Dr.Eleni Arapaki, the AP Physics 1 and Teacher at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, teams of three high school students work together to solve problems by using JAVA, Python, C#, and/or C++ programming to complete the “quest”.

title: Live Feed | Bridgewater-Raritan High School
content: chool, teams of three high school students work together to solve problems by using JAVA, Python, C#, and/or C++ programming to complete the “quest”. The problem set consists of 20 to 30 challenging problems created by Lockheed Martin engineers and computer programmers. The students have to solve as many problems as possible within a 2.5-hour time limit by writing software programs that produce a predetermined output from a given set of input. US-based attendees at these events are offered the opportunity to apply for one of several summer internships across the Lockheed Martin corporation. Bridgewater-Raritan High School participated in the Code Quest held on March 1 in Lockheed Martin’s facility in Moorestown. Dr. Arapaki and Ken Mascola, the Advanced Topics in Computer Science Teacher at Bridgewater-Raritan High School, brought three teams to compete in the advanced division. The Greek Gods (Anthony Channg, Ian Francis, and Foteini Gallou) placed 8th on site, the Eastern Engineers (Arya Vadya, Luvyakrish Manche, and Navneet Kalatheeswaran) placed 6th, and The Void Team (Jeffrey Hu, Ethan Pear, and Anika Seshan) ranked fourth.

s (Arya Vadya, Luvyakrish Manche, and Navneet Kalatheeswaran) placed 6th, and The Void Team (Jeffrey Hu, Ethan Pear, and Anika Seshan) ranked fourth. "Congratulations to all our kids for their dedication and hard work," Dr. Arapaki said.
Dr. Arapaki reported, “during the event, the students and the teachers had the opportunity to attend presentations on information about Lockheed Martin scholarships and internships, as well as career opportunities on AI & machine learning, advanced manufacturing, cybersecurity, engineering & technology, finance & business operations, IT, and technical operations” 10 months ago, Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District The Bridgewater-Raritan High School Theatre Company Class and THS Troupe #5154 will present a collection of one-act plays on Friday, June 6. Bridgewater-Raritan High School Credit And Course Requirements For Graduation

BRHS Program of Studies page 2
Educational Philosophy
The educational philosophy of the Bridgewater-Raritan Regional School District is founded on the belief that the school's
responsibility is to cultivate intellect, civic-mindedness, and sociability in students through the combined efforts of staff,
administration, parents, and community. Our Board of Education believes that students should be guided to attain the
knowledge and develop the skills necessary for success in future occupational endeavors and the social world in which
they will find themselves after formal schooling ends.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
he skills necessary for success in future occupational endeavors and the social world in which
they will find themselves after formal schooling ends. A fundamental aim of our schools is to develop in youth an
understanding of, an appreciation for, and a devotion to the democratic way of life, and to instill a desire to improve it.
The Board believes each individual's welfare and development are the basis of our educational program. All children shall
have an opportunity to learn. The goal is to be a well-prepared individual who can meet and solve society's problems.
The Board and staff accept the following charges to achieve this goal:
● To accept primary responsibility for giving students a mastery of the basic skills of learning, thinking, and problem
solving; for teaching them to use the various media of self-expression; for instilling in them a knowledge of the
social and natural sciences; for acquainting them with the richness of our heritage; and for stimulating them to
productive work in the various areas of human endeavor.
● To recognize their responsibility to help meet the physical, intellectual, and emotional needs of children,

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
in the various areas of human endeavor.
● To recognize their responsibility to help meet the physical, intellectual, and emotional needs of children,
particularly the needs to inquire, to learn, to think, to create, to establish aesthetic, moral, and ethical values, and
to relate satisfactorily to others in social situations involving family, work, government, and recreation.
● To acknowledge the importance of their supplemental role to the home and other social agencies in developing
habits that make for practical personal living, maintaining optimum physical and mental health, and establishing
sound moral, ethical, and aesthetic values.

BRHS Program of Studies page 3
Graduation — Requirements
Diplomas shall be granted only to students who have completed the requirements for graduation as mandated by state
law and administrative code established and approved by the Bridgewater-Raritan Board of Education.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

requirements for graduation as mandated by state
law and administrative code established and approved by the Bridgewater-Raritan Board of Education. Those
requirements include:
● The achievement of approved proficiency levels in all courses necessary to satisfy graduation requirements.
● The student's compliance with the district's attendance requirements as stated in BOE Regulation 5200, Attendance.
● Students in the Class of 2027 must complete either the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) or the New
Jersey Alternative Financial Aid Application unless an opt-out waiver is completed. The waiver must be signed by the
parent or guardian or by the student if aged eighteen (18) or older, or by the student’s School Counselor if the
student is under the age of seventeen (17), and a form signed by the parent/guardian cannot be reasonably attained.
● The achievement of a passing score on the New Jersey Graduation Proficiency Assessment (NJGPA) in Grade 11.

and Geometry.

AP/Honors Program
The Advanced Placement (AP) Program, in cooperation with the College Board, extends educational opportunities to
students who wish to be challenged by college-level courses.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
AP) Program, in cooperation with the College Board, extends educational opportunities to
students who wish to be challenged by college-level courses. Appropriate credit and placement at many colleges may
occur as a result of scores achieved on Advanced Placement Examinations.
Currently, Advanced Placement courses are available in 2D Art & Design, Art History, Biology, Business with Personal
Finance, Calculus, Chemistry, Chinese, Computer Science, Computer Science Principles, Cybersecurity, English,
Environmental Science, European History, French, Government & Politics, Human Geography, Italian, Latin,
Macroeconomics, Microeconomics, Music Theory, Physics, Statistics, Psychology, Research, Seminar, Spanish, and United
States History. Students accepted into the Advanced Placement courses are expected to take the AP examination at the
end of the year. Registration for an AP exam is contingent upon BRHS course enrollment.
Retention in Advanced Placement/Honors courses follows the guidelines defined in the next section.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
AP exam is contingent upon BRHS course enrollment.
Retention in Advanced Placement/Honors courses follows the guidelines defined in the next section. Students enrolling in
Advanced Placement courses that do not have a respective academic course and seek to drop the Advanced Placement
course after the allotted time in the drop/change procedures are eligible for a withdrawal (W) or may remain in the AP
course to earn a grade higher than a failure.
AP/Honors courses are available in the following subjects: English, World Languages, Mathematics, Science, Visual Arts,
Business, Performing Arts, and Social Studies. These courses offer enrichment in both the scope and exploration of the
material presented.
Multiple factors will be considered for placement into AP/Honors Courses, including but not limited to:
● PSAT Score
● AP Exam Score
● AP Potential Recommendation
● Prerequisite Course Grade
● Appeals will be considered
Students who wish to enroll in AP/Honors-level courses must meet eligibility criteria as indicated below:
❖ Middle school students enrolled in “E” level courses and maintaining a grade of “B-” or higher are eligible to enroll in

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

iteria as indicated below:

the corresponding high school Honors-level course.
❖ Middle school students enrolled in “Gen Ed”- level courses and maintaining an “A-” or higher average can enroll in the
corresponding high school Honors-level course.
❖ Students with an AP Potential course recommendation.
❖ Students currently enrolled in an AP course and maintaining a “B-“ or higher average are eligible to continue
enrollment in next year’s AP course in the corresponding subject area.

enrollment in next year’s corresponding AP level course
❖ Students currently enrolled in an Honors course and maintaining a “C+” or higher average are eligible to continue
enrollment in next year’s Honors course in the corresponding subject area.
❖ Students currently enrolled in an Honors course and maintaining a “B+” or higher average are eligible to advance into
next year’s AP course in the corresponding subject area.

ed in an Honors course and maintaining a “B+” or higher average are eligible to advance into

Retention in AP/Honors Level Courses
Students in grades 9-12 enrolled in an AP/Honors course are responsible for maintaining a grade of at least a “B-“ in that
course. The following level changes, from AP to Honors, from AP to Academic, and from Honors to Academic, are
permitted until October 14, 2026.
All students should be aware of the following regarding AP/Honors level course changes:
Level Changes
❖ Level change requests will be considered in accordance with the following process:
1. The student was enrolled in the course for a minimum of 3 weeks.
2.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
l change requests will be considered in accordance with the following process:
1. The student was enrolled in the course for a minimum of 3 weeks.
2. The student completed course assignments, tests, etc., as expected.
3. The student communicated concerns with the classroom teacher.
4. The student sought extra help with the classroom teacher and other available resources (e.g., tutor)
5. Meet with assigned School Counselor. If determined that a level change should be requested, the School
Counselor will provide the student with the Level Change Form
6. Student completes the form and submits the completed form no later than October 14, 2026.
7. The form is reviewed by the department-level supervisor, who will render a decision.
If approved, the unweighted grade earned will follow from the more rigorous level course to the less
rigorous level course. After this period, students who have not opted for a level change will remain in their current
course-level placement for the duration of the school year.

❖ Continued eligibility for the next school year in an AP-level course is contingent upon receiving a final grade
(transcript grade) of “B-“ or higher in the corresponding subject area. Students earning a final “C+” or lower grade
will be ineligible to continue in that subject area unless they receive a 3 or higher on the AP Exam in that course.
❖ Continued eligibility for the next school year in an Honors-level course is contingent upon receiving a final grade
(transcript grade) of “C+“ or higher in the corresponding subject area. Students earning a final grade of “C” or lower
will be ineligible to continue in that subject area.

BRHS Program of Studies page 8
AP Capstone Diploma™
AP Capstone™ is a diploma program based on two AP courses: AP Seminar and AP Research. These yearlong
courses focus on developing the critical thinking, research, collaboration, time management, and presentation skills
needed for college-level work. AP Capstone™ fosters the research, argumentation, and communication skills at the core
of college readiness and lifelong learning. AP Capstone™ lets students demonstrate to colleges that they have
challenged themselves academically and illustrate their passion and interest in specific research topics. Students may earn
an AP Capstone™ designation on his/her diploma following the successful completion of AP Seminar, AP Research
[earned AP Exam score of three (3) or higher on each], and the successful completion of any combination of four (4) AP

BRHS Program of Studies page 9
Option II
The New Jersey Department of Education (NJDOE) recognizes that not all students will achieve the New Jersey Student
Learning Standards (NJSLS) in the same manner or with the same level of success. To this end, the Bridgewater-Raritan
Regional School District provides students with individualized learning opportunities outside the traditional classroom that
are stimulating and challenging, enabling them to meet or exceed the New Jersey Student Learning Standards. This is
commonly referred to as “Option II.” Option II allows for the design and implementation of programs to meet the needs
of all students. Students may earn credit toward graduation through Option II learning experiences. Credits toward
graduation will be awarded only to students currently enrolled in high school. Enrollment begins in September of their
freshman year.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
Credits toward
graduation will be awarded only to students currently enrolled in high school. Enrollment begins in September of their
freshman year. These experiences include, but are not limited to, interdisciplinary or theme-based programs, independent
study, early college credit, magnet programs, student exchange programs, distance learning, online learning, work-based
programs, internships, service learning, co-curricular or extra-curricular programs, and/or other structured learning
experiences. In addition, Option II allows for group programs based upon specific instructional objectives that meet or
exceed New Jersey Student Learning Standards. Participation in Option II is predicated on the application process through
which students seek approval. The process for application, evaluation, and assessment is detailed below.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
redicated on the application process through
which students seek approval. The process for application, evaluation, and assessment is detailed below. Attainment of
credit toward graduation is based on the successful completion of assessments that verify student achievement in
meeting or exceeding the New Jersey Student Learning Standards at the high school level.
Option II Credit Attainment
Students planning to pursue coursework for credit external to the traditional offerings of the BRHS curriculum are required
to submit a completed Option II application. Deadlines for submission are June 1st for Summer and Fall Semester
coursework and January 1st for Spring Semester coursework. Each application will be reviewed to determine eligibility
and grant approval/disapproval based on the criteria outlined. Each student’s application will be reviewed on its merit. A
student may apply under Option II for the following alternatives:
● Additional/Original credit
● Accelerated/Original Credit
● College Credit
● Alternate Physical Education
● Credit Recovery
● Independent Study
● Service Learning
The application/approval process will proceed as follows:
1.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
● Alternate Physical Education
● Credit Recovery
● Independent Study
● Service Learning
The application/approval process will proceed as follows:
1. The student meets with the assigned school counselor to discuss the proposal. If viable, the school counselor will
invite the student to complete an electronic Option II application.
2. The application will then be reviewed by the department Supervisor, who may approve, deny, or specify additional
requirements necessary for approval.
3. The building principal will determine final approval.
4. Upon completion of the approval process, a final confirmation message will be sent to all individuals involved.
Option II Guidelines
● Students are not permitted to enroll in courses that are graduation requirements. Exceptions may be permitted on
a case-by-case basis due to BRHS schedule conflicts/limitations.

calculation of a student’s overall Grade Point Average (GPA). Official transcripts generated by coursework taken outside of

Additional Credit; Acceleration Credit
A student may seek approval to enroll in an Option II course to earn additional credit and/or to accelerate a course level.
Additional credit or acceleration can be achieved through in-person or online coursework. The following guidelines must
be followed:
1. Graduation requirements are not eligible for acceleration or additional credit.
2. The course must be from an accredited institution. Additional/Acceleration Credit courses must meet the
120-hour enrollment requirement for complete course advancement and meet or exceed NJ Standards.
3. The course must be approved through the process outlined above.

13, 2026. Acceleration will be limited to those who have met the standard BRHS grade requirements for
prerequisite courses, have completed an approved course, and have earned the minimum BRHS Final Exam

5. Additional Credit courses must be completed within 9 (nine) months and prior to June 4, 2027. An official
transcript from the institution must be submitted promptly following the completion of the course.
College Credit
Students may seek approval to enroll in college-level courses and apply credits earned toward high school graduation
requirements. Students enrolling in college-level courses must meet eligibility requirements established by the college or
university. The following guidelines must be followed:
1. The course must be taken from a regionally accredited two or four-year college/university.

An official transcript from the college/university must be submitted promptly following the completion of the course.
Earned grades will appear on the transcript, but will not be factored into the student’s GPA.
Credit Recovery
Students receiving a final grade of “U” in a BRHS course do not receive credit. Students who fail to meet course
requirements and/or fail to meet attendance requirements receive a final letter grade with an asterisk designation, e.g.,
“B*”. In each case, credits toward graduation are not earned. Student options include repeating the course during the
next school year or enrolling in an approved Option II summer program. Completed applications must be submitted by
June 30, 2026. Registration for summer credit recovery courses must be completed by July 8, 2026. This includes
traditional classroom and online credit recovery courses. All summer credit recovery courses must be completed by
August 6, 2026.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

traditional classroom and online credit recovery courses. All summer credit recovery courses must be completed by
August 6, 2026. Students enrolled in an online credit recovery course must take the final exam for that course at BRHS.
Final exams are available by appointment, weekdays only – August 3 - 6, 2026.
Credit Recovery courses require students to be enrolled for a minimum of 60 hours for a 5-credit course. Students are
permitted to enroll in a maximum of 2 (two) Credit Recovery courses during the summer. Grades for completed Credit
Recovery courses will be reflected on transcripts with the designation of “P” (Pass) or “U” (Fail). Approved courses
dropped after the drop/add period will be recorded on BRHS transcripts as “W” (Withdrawn). Credit recovery courses are
not included in calculating a student’s overall GPA. The original course and final grade will be retained on the student’s
transcript.

Alternate Physical Education
Students may pursue opportunities to meet the New Jersey Student Learning Standards for Physical Education through
participation in co-curricular or extra-curricular programs and/or through other structured learning experiences that
involve substantial physical activity/exertion. Students should apply for consideration through the Option II process as
indicated above (Option II Guidelines and Deadlines). Students participating in activities outside of BRHS must
submit a written letter on organizational letterhead, with a handwritten signature of the coach or advisor, verifying that
the student will participate in a minimum of 150 minutes per week (Mon-Sun) in said physical activity. If approval is given,
the student will be assigned a study hall during the period that would be scheduled for Physical Education during the
marking period(s) for which an alternative experience has been approved.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
during the period that would be scheduled for Physical Education during the
marking period(s) for which an alternative experience has been approved. Students are responsible for maintaining an
activity log that documents their hours and is signed by the coach or advisor. Students are also responsible for submitting
weekly journals documenting their progress in meeting the physical education content standards. The activity logs and
journals must be submitted to their assigned Physical Education teacher every week. (More information is available on the
high school website – BRHS Option II Phys Ed). The student will be required to participate in the health curriculum
during the marking period during which health is scheduled.

BRHS Program of Studies page 12
NCAA - Option II
Nontraditional courses are taught online or through distance learning, hybrid/blended, independent study, individualized
instruction, correspondence, or similar means.
Generally, for a nontraditional course to count as an NCAA-approved core course, it must meet all of the following
requirements:
● The course must meet all requirements for an NCAA-approved core course.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
NCAA-approved core course, it must meet all of the following
requirements:
● The course must meet all requirements for an NCAA-approved core course.
● Students in the course must have regular instructor-led interaction for instruction, evaluation, and assistance
throughout the course. This may include, for example, exchanging emails between the student and teacher,
online chats, phone calls, feedback on assignments, and the opportunity for the teacher to engage the student in
individual or group instruction.
● The course must have a defined completion time. For example, it should be clear how long students must remain
enrolled and working in the course, and how long a school would permit a student to work on a single
nontraditional course.
● Student work (e.g., exams, papers, assignments) must be available for evaluation and validation.
● The course should be clearly identified as nontraditional on the student’s official high school transcript.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
vailable for evaluation and validation.
● The course should be clearly identified as nontraditional on the student’s official high school transcript. It is
important to note that nontraditional courses are displayed as Pass/Fail on BRHS transcripts and
will be assigned a grade of “D” by the NCAA Eligibility Center when calculating a student’s
grade-point average (GPA).
National Honor Society
Membership in the National Honor Society is an honor bestowed upon a student. Selection for membership is by a faculty
council and is based on leadership, service, character, and outstanding scholarship (minimum 3.5 grade point average,
rounded to the hundredths on a 4.33 weighted scale; all courses are factored into computing the grade point average).
NOTE: Refer to the student handbook for further information.

BRHS Program of Studies page 13
BR Career Concentrations
What are career concentrations?
Career concentrations are multi-year coherent course progressions that provide opportunities for students to design an
educational program aligned with their possible post-secondary college and career interests.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
ons that provide opportunities for students to design an
educational program aligned with their possible post-secondary college and career interests. Detailed information is
available on the high school website: Career Concentrations.
How can I enroll?
Enrollment opportunities are available via PowerSchool's Parent/Student Portal. Enrollment is also available through your
assigned School Counselor. The information here is meant to help students plan their courses if they are interested in
enrolling in a career concentration.
What will students do in a career concentration?
To complete a career concentration, students must take a minimum of 17.5 credits from the available courses for that
concentration, including a capstone course in which they will complete a culminating project related to their
concentration.
Which careers do we have career concentrations for?
The following career concentrations are currently in place: 3D Design & Production, Business, Computer Science,
Engineering & Robotics, Media & Content Creation, Music, and Visual Arts.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

Engineering & Robotics, Media & Content Creation, Music, and Visual Arts. The course options for those concentrations
are available below.
3D Design & Production Concentration
While these courses span diverse disciplines, they share a fundamental commonality: each requires students to
engage in practical problem-solving for real-world applications through a structured planning and production process.
Every course in this concentration addresses authentic problems that professionals encounter daily. This real-world
relevance ensures that students develop technical skills alongside the critical thinking, project management, and
problem-solving abilities that transfer across careers and contexts.

BRHS Program of Studies page 17
BRHS Dual Enrollment
Opportunities are available to Sophomore, Junior, and Senior students to earn college credit through Fairleigh Dickinson
University, Raritan Valley Community College, Rutgers University, Seton Hall University, and The College of New Jersey
(TCNJ). Dual enrollment allows eligible students to earn college credits for high school courses aligned with the
designated college/university course. BRHS offers semester and full-year dual enrollment courses taught by BRHS faculty
on the high school campus. Students may earn 3 to 5 college credits per course, depending on the course and the
post-secondary institution.
College credits earned can be transferred to other post-secondary institutions; however, decisions regarding credit
transfers and their application are at the discretion of the individual institution.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
post-secondary institutions; however, decisions regarding credit
transfers and their application are at the discretion of the individual institution. For more information about in-state (New
Jersey) credit transfers, visit www.njtransfer.org. You are also encouraged to contact the admissions office of any
college/university you consider attending.
Dual enrollment costs range from $240 - $660 per course, depending on credit value and the institution. Please note that
participation begins at BRHS to ensure students are enrolled in the correct course. Registration procedures and payment
with the college/university follow student enrollment in the BRHS course. You must notify your assigned School Counselor
of your intent to enroll in and register for a dual enrollment course.
A complete list of BRHS dual enrollment courses, partnering colleges/universities, their corresponding course(s), and
associated costs is listed below.

Preparation for College Entrance
The philosophies of education, specific course requirements, and other qualifications for acceptance vary among the
nation's colleges and universities.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
e philosophies of education, specific course requirements, and other qualifications for acceptance vary among the
nation's colleges and universities. All, however, recognize the desirability of a broad education with a strong foundation in
traditional, solid academic subjects.
Students are evaluated for admission not only on the basis of their grades but also on the strength of the academic
program they have undertaken. Students are encouraged to take the strongest possible academic program available
within his/her own personal limitations during all four years of high school. A rigorous secondary school preparation
remains the best means for a student to maximize the benefits of a challenging college education.
NCAA College-Bound Student-Athletes
The National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) has specific academic requirements for students to be eligible to
participate in athletics at the Division I or II collegiate level. It is the responsibility of the student and parent/guardian to
ensure compliance with all NCAA eligibility requirements. It is recommended that students/parents/guardians consult with
the Counseling Department beginning with 9th-grade course selection.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
ity requirements. It is recommended that students/parents/guardians consult with
the Counseling Department beginning with 9th-grade course selection. NCAA eligibility requirements are based on course
enrollment (core courses) and core-course grade point average (GPA). Minimum requirements are based on
collegiate-level play – Division I or II. Please be aware that courses taken outside of BRHS that are not on the approved
48-H form (courses approved by the NCAA for BRHS students) will not count for NCAA eligibility. It is strongly advised that
any potential college athlete check the NCAA Website for information on Option II programs and NCAA eligibility.
For more information, please visit the NCAA Eligibility website at:
NCAA Eligibility Center
You may also access a quick reference sheet of initial eligibility requirements at:
NCAA Initial Eligibility Standards
Class Rank
The academic environment in Bridgewater-Raritan Regional High School is very challenging. The majority of our students
meet that challenge by earning exemplary grades.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
onment in Bridgewater-Raritan Regional High School is very challenging. The majority of our students
meet that challenge by earning exemplary grades. However, we believe the comparisons among students inherent in
rank-in-class calculations unnecessarily increase competition within the school; further, we believe that our students’ levels
of achievement are not equitably or fully communicated by this single transcript statistic. Therefore,
Bridgewater-Raritan High School procedures preclude the reporting of class rank for college applications.
Note that upon college acceptance, class rank will be made available upon request by the
college/university to determine financial aid and scholarship eligibility. This information will only be made
available to the inquiring institution.
Master Schedule
The high school’s master schedule and staffing patterns are determined by students' course selections. Every attempt is
made to accommodate each student’s requests. However, due to the complexities inherent in building a master schedule
for a school of our size, conflicts arise, leaving the school unable to fulfill all requests.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
the complexities inherent in building a master schedule
for a school of our size, conflicts arise, leaving the school unable to fulfill all requests. Board of Education policy (2312,
Class Size) indicates that each high school course must have a minimum of 15 students to be offered. There may be
occasions when a course will not be offered due to insufficient enrollment. This will require selecting an alternate request.
Students are given the opportunity to resolve scheduling conflicts during the summer before the start of school. Following
the start of school, schedule changes must follow the procedures outlined below.

BRHS Program of Studies page 20
Scheduling Procedures/Timeline
The process of selecting an academic program involves many people and requires several months of preparation and
consultation. Class size considerations and staffing are determined by initial student course requests. Student course
selections are made after serious deliberation among students, parents, faculty, and counselors.

The BRHS Counseling Department will be conducting a BRHS Scheduling presentation for parents of current BRMS
8th-grade students on Tuesday, February 10, 2026, in the BRHS Auditorium. BRMS School Counselors will be presenting
to 8th-grade students in January and February to explain high school schedules, available high school courses, graduation
requirements, and the online registration process. BRHS School Counselors will conduct classroom presentations during all
Junior, Sophomore, and Freshman English classes from February 9 – 13, 2026.
All students will engage in an online registration process in which core (English, Mathematics, Science, Social Studies)
content and elective course requests are selected by the individual student. Incoming freshmen (current BRMS 8th-grade
students) and current BRHS students (Juniors, Sophomores, and Freshmen) will register for courses during the week of
February 13 - 23, 2026.

Summer Schedule Changes
Student schedules will be released via the Parent Portal in PowerSchool by mid-August. Please note that the schedule
received is tentative and subject to change without notice. The basis for such changes is systemic needs, e.g., to balance
course sections, implement programmatic changes, and adjust staffing patterns. Once courses have been selected,
program changes are recognized only under special circumstances. Scheduling problems and errors, as well as course
conflicts, are given the highest priority.

Dropping a course is a serious matter and is generally discouraged; it should be taken only after careful thought and
consultation. The request to drop a course must be in writing from the parent or guardian, unless the student is their own

Auditing Courses*
● Students will be given the privilege of auditing courses under the following circumstances:
1. Permission from the parent
2. Permission from the teacher
3. Permission from the department supervisor
4. Permission from the counselor
● Requests to audit semester courses will be considered through the seventh week of the semester.
● Requests to audit full-year courses will be considered through the twelfth week of the course.
● Courses audited will be noted on report cards and transcripts in the grade column as “AU.”
*Auditors have the same responsibilities in the class as any other student and may not detract from the
performance of the regular students in the class.

BRHS Program of Studies page 23
COURSE DESCRIPTIONS
NOTE: The first-grade level listed under each course indicates the year the course first becomes available. Additional
grade levels show years of continuing student interest.
English Language & Literature
Overview
● All students must satisfactorily complete four years of English Language & Literature.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
ing student interest.
English Language & Literature
Overview
● All students must satisfactorily complete four years of English Language & Literature. This requirement means that all
students must complete all core requirements for English Language & Literature (5 credits) each year they attend
school.
● Students who are required to take Literacy Support in addition to English Language & Literature courses are identified
based on district- and state-administered test scores.
● In addition to the four years required for English Language & Literature, students may select additional courses from
the list of electives. These courses may not replace any of the four years of required English Language & Literature.
● The English Language & Literature courses are characterized by a strong emphasis on the relationship between
language processes and thinking and learning. Through the structured integration of speaking, reading, listening, and
viewing activities, students can master basic skills and develop a sustained interest in language and literature.
● Flexible Grouping. Instruction takes place through large groups, small groups, and independent learning.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
ained interest in language and literature.
● Flexible Grouping. Instruction takes place through large groups, small groups, and independent learning. Flexible
grouping and varied activities help maintain interest in learning and accommodate individual differences.
● Speaking. To encourage maximum participation, the courses include a wide variety of informal and formal speaking
activities, such as oral reports and the dramatization of stories. Speaking is also taught as a vital part of the
composing process and is related closely to the development of listening skills.
● Writing. Writing is viewed as a continuing, rather than an occasional, activity that requires writing in varied, realistic,
challenging ways. Writing is taught not only for its own value but also as a means of thinking and learning. Central to
the writing program is the composing process, in which students learn to prepare, write, and revise their work for a
given audience and purpose.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
l to
the writing program is the composing process, in which students learn to prepare, write, and revise their work for a
given audience and purpose. Writing assignments range from personal responses to formal research papers.
Instruction in the support skills - grammar, usage, and mechanics - figures prominently in the editing phase of the
composing process.
● Literature and Reading. The study of literature is diverse and challenging. The selection of contemporary and
classic literature includes the study of the major genres: novel, drama, short story, biography, and poem. Students are
provided with materials to meet their reading ability and personal interests. Literature is taught in response to
grade-level essential questions either through thematic units or through American, British, or world selections. All of
these encourage personal, literal, inferential, critical, and creative responses.
● Viewing.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
r through American, British, or world selections. All of
these encourage personal, literal, inferential, critical, and creative responses.
● Viewing. Since the non-print media - especially films and television - are major factors in shaping people’s lives,
students are taught to read visual images and messages intelligently and to share their responses with others in
speech and writing.

English Department Course Descriptions
Advanced Placement Language and Composition III Credits: 5
Grade 11
This college-level course in effective writing and critical reading centers on fostering a student’s awareness of writing as a
thinking process. Intense concentration on language use and stylistic development will lead to insight regarding
interpretative reading and writing skills.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
ng process. Intense concentration on language use and stylistic development will lead to insight regarding
interpretative reading and writing skills. The fiction and nonfiction course readings will serve as tools for advancing the
manipulation of language details and structure to create meaning primarily within the modes of exposition, analysis, and
argumentation.
**Students completing Honors English II in good academic standing may elect Advanced Placement Language and
Composition III.
Advanced Placement Literature and Composition IV Credits: 5
Grade 12
This college-level course enables students to focus on intensive, in-depth, advanced study in English. A critical analysis of
the literature will yield insights into structure and meaning. Authors and literary works from the 16th century to the
present will be studied.

English Language and Literature I (A) Credits: 5
Grade 9
Language and Literature I focuses on classic and contemporary literature to establish a framework for students to use
language actively to explore the world, communicate effectively, and grow intellectually. Through reading, writing,
speaking, listening, viewing, and critical thinking, learning becomes a multidimensional, transactional process through
which students continually expand their knowledge repertoire.

Grade 10
Language and Literature II adds to the framework of prior knowledge through a study of American literature, encouraging
