English Department Electives
Advanced Creative Writing Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Introduction to Creative Writing
This course is designed for students who demonstrate a strong interest and aptitude in creative writing. In a structured
workshop setting, students will write, revise, and refine original fiction and poetry to produce a polished final portfolio.
Students will study exemplary works across genres and participate in regular peer critique, learning the techniques and
expectations of a professional writing community.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
exemplary works across genres and participate in regular peer critique, learning the techniques and
expectations of a professional writing community. Open to students who have completed Introduction to Creative Writing,
this half-year course emphasizes sustained development, revision, and growth as confident, working writers.
Available as a Dual Enrollment course through Seton Hall University.
Introduction to Creative Writing (Media & Content Creation Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Introduction to Creative Writing teaches you the process involved in writing fiction, drama, and poetry. As you are
introduced to each new form of writing, you will read literature that illustrates the type of writing you are doing.
Beginning with short assignments to sharpen your use of sensory perception and imagery, you will eventually progress to
writing character sketches, short stories, one-act plays, and poetry.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
pen your use of sensory perception and imagery, you will eventually progress to
writing character sketches, short stories, one-act plays, and poetry. You will be encouraged to share your creative work
with others.

BRHS Program of Studies page 26
Kid Lit Literacy Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
This course explores the history of children’s literature, focusing on societal, cultural, and technological influences.
Additionally, the course demonstrates how specific children’s literature can be used as a learning tool across all academic
subjects at the high school level. The culmination of the course will be an original production of a picture book.
Literacy Support 12 Credits: 2.5
Grades 12
This offering is required for all students who do not meet state graduation standards in reading and/or writing, as well as
for students identified through the English department’s student performance monitoring system. This course involves
creating an individual-student learning diagnosis, differentiating instruction based on a unique master student
prescription, and administering instructional strategies that drive learning.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
diagnosis, differentiating instruction based on a unique master student
prescription, and administering instructional strategies that drive learning. An awareness of each student’s learning
profile, based on the systems that affect output, guides literacy instruction in guided, independent, and applied practice
formations.
Lyrical Poetry Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
From Beethoven to the Beastie Boys to the Grateful Dead and the Dave Matthews Band, LP: Lyrical Poetry provides
students with a solid foundation in the formal analysis of literature, music, and critical terminology. Each area of this class
will strive to create an understanding and appreciation of the historical, aesthetic, and cultural aspects of lyrical poetry.
Furthermore, this course will allow students to strengthen and build upon their poetic/lyrical writing styles, both formal
and creative.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
ical poetry.
Furthermore, this course will allow students to strengthen and build upon their poetic/lyrical writing styles, both formal
and creative. Ultimately, students will gain a greater appreciation for writing poetry and lyrics through their own unique
experiences.
SAT Preparation Credits: 2.5
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite - Algebra II or Essentials of Algebra II, or currently taking
This elective course focuses on math and verbal SAT preparation for students planning to take the SAT. It is designed to
increase student awareness of important test-taking strategies and, more importantly, to enhance higher-order
problem-solving and thinking skills. One marking period will be devoted to verbal skills and one to math skills.
Stephen King & American Horror Credits: 2.5
Grades 11, 12
In this introductory unit of study, students will take a deep dive into many of the works of American horror writer Stephen
King. Students will explore the literary elements Stephen King employs and the cultural impact of the horror genre on
society. Furthermore, the focus will be on classifying, identifying, and defining American horror pieces through objective
analysis.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
horror genre on
society. Furthermore, the focus will be on classifying, identifying, and defining American horror pieces through objective
analysis. In addition, we will explore the proper use of specific, focused, and appropriate language and jargon in the
horror genre.

BRHS Program of Studies page 27
Mathematics
The high school mathematics curriculum is designed to meet individual differences in ability, interests, and future
educational/life goals. Students are required to take 15 credits of high school mathematics, including Algebra I and
Geometry, or their content equivalents, and a third-year math course that builds on the concepts and skills of Algebra I
and Geometry and prepares students for college and 21st-century careers. Courses in statistics, engineering, and
computer science are also available through the mathematics course offerings.

This course is designed to prepare students for the College Board Advanced Placement Calculus AB Exam to seek college

This course is designed to prepare students for the College Board Advanced Placement Calculus BC Exam to seek college
credit and/or advanced standing in mathematics.

Advanced Placement Probability and Statistics is offered to students who wish to prepare for the College Board Advanced
Placement Examination to seek college credit and/or advanced standing in mathematics. Students will be encouraged to
take the examination in May. The content of this course will include introductory yet comprehensive material on topics
such as data exploration, observational studies, experiments, surveys, probability, and statistical inference. Problems from
a variety of disciplines will provide connections between statistics and real-life applications.

Algebra I (A) Credits: 5
Grade 9
This course will develop the necessary skills in algebra in order to connect algebra to geometry, connect algebra to
probability and statistics, connect ideas between mathematics and other disciplines, learn how to study mathematics,
develop the ability to explore, conjecture, and reason logically, solve non-routine problems, and communicate about and
through mathematics. Topics include variables, addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division in algebra; slopes and
lines; exponents and powers; quadratic equations and square roots; polynomials; linear systems; and factoring.

This course will emphasize problem-solving, communication, reasoning, and connections. The following topics will be
explored: analyzing equations and inequalities; graphing linear relations and functions; solving systems of linear equations
and inequalities; using matrices, polynomials, and radical expressions; quadratic functions and inequalities; polynomial
functions; rational expressions; and exponential and logarithmic functions. The honors course moves at an accelerated
pace, and the work is more challenging.

Algebra II Integrated with Data Science Credits 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisites - Algebra I, Geometry
In this course, concepts from Algebra I and Geometry are extended to the functions typically seen in an Algebra II course
(polynomial, logarithmic, rational, trigonometric), with Data Science infused throughout. Data Science uses an
inquiry-based approach to examine real-world issues across the social sciences, economics, and the environment, to
name a few. Data is gathered and analyzed for trends and relationships, and the validity of those data-collection methods
is studied.
This represents the starting pathway course toward Statistics.

Elements of Algebra and Trigonometry is designed to strengthen and extend students’ algebraic concepts and skills in
preparation for precalculus/calculus. The course introduces the study of trigonometric functions, identities, equations, and
their applications.

Statistics (A) Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite – Geometry
This course will cover a broad range of topics in applied statistics and probability, equipping students to make more
informed decisions through the analysis of quantitative data. Understanding probability and statistics is essential in today’s
high-tech world, where print and electronic media are full of numerical information and interpretation. Statistics is the
mathematics we use to collect, organize, and interpret numerical data, from test scores to election results to shoppers’
product preferences.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
is the
mathematics we use to collect, organize, and interpret numerical data, from test scores to election results to shoppers’
product preferences. Probability is the study of uncertainty and assessing the likelihood of real-world events occurring,
whether it is games of chance, genetics, or weather prediction, to anticipate and prepare for a major storm.
Available as a Dual Enrollment course through Fairleigh Dickinson or Seton Hall University.
Precalculus (Honors or A) Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Algebra II
This full-year course lays the groundwork for calculus by providing essential concepts and skills in algebra, trigonometry,
and functions. Numerous real-world applications will be discussed. The honors course moves at an accelerated pace, and
the work is more challenging. The honors course devotes more time to factorable, reducible, and quadratic relations;
sequences and series; probability; and limits.
Available as a Dual Enrollment course through RVCC or Seton Hall University.
Calculus (A) Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite - Precalculus
This course includes the material normally taught in the first two semesters of a college calculus program.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite - Precalculus
This course includes the material normally taught in the first two semesters of a college calculus program. It deals with
the fundamentals of differential and integral calculus. After a review of analytic geometry, limit theory is developed. The
properties of limits are used to find the derivatives of algebraic and transcendental functions. Applications of derivatives
are then discussed. The concept of integration is introduced, and the Fundamental Theorem of Integral Calculus is used
as a basis for the application of the definite integral. Greater versatility in integration is provided by studying the methods
of integration.
Available as a Dual Enrollment course through RVCC or Seton Hall University.

course towards solving more complex single differential equations and systems of differential equations.

The AP Computer Science Principles course is designed to be equivalent to a first-semester introductory college
computing course.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
e II, OR Algebra II
The AP Computer Science Principles course is designed to be equivalent to a first-semester introductory college
computing course. In this course, students will develop computational thinking skills vital for success across all disciplines,
such as using computational tools to analyze data and working with large data sets to visualize and draw conclusions from
trends. The course is unique in its focus on fostering student creativity. Students are encouraged to apply creative
processes when developing computational artifacts and to think creatively when using software and other technologies to
explore questions that interest them. They will also develop effective communication and collaboration skills, working
individually and collaboratively to solve problems and discussing and writing about the importance of these problems and
their impacts on their community, society, and the world.
Advanced Placement Cybersecurity (Computer Science Career Concentration course) Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Cybersecurity I
AP Cybersecurity is a broad introduction to the field of cybersecurity that aligns closely with a standard first-year college

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
isite - Cybersecurity I
AP Cybersecurity is a broad introduction to the field of cybersecurity that aligns closely with a standard first-year college
introductory cybersecurity course. Students learn about common threats and vulnerabilities, and how those combine to
create risk. Students study how individuals and organizations manage risk and how risk can be mitigated through a
defense-in-depth strategy. Students explore specific vulnerabilities, attacks, mitigations, and detection measures across a
variety of domains, including physical spaces, computer networks, devices, and data and applications. Throughout the
course, students consider the impact of cybersecurity on individuals, organizations, governments, and societies.

BRHS Program of Studies page 33
Advanced Topics in Computer Science* (Computer Science Career Concentration course) Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite - AP Computer Science A
Advanced Topics in Computer Science is a course that follows AP Computer Science A to investigate the essential
properties of data structures and algorithms for operating on them, to use these structures as tools to assist algorithm

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
to investigate the essential
properties of data structures and algorithms for operating on them, to use these structures as tools to assist algorithm
design, and to extend exposure to searching, sorting, and hashing techniques. Topics covered during the year include
linked lists, stacks, queues, binary trees, sequential search, binary search, binary search trees, balanced binary search
trees, hash tables, heaps for priority queues, graphs, and graph algorithms, and asymptotic analysis of time and space
requirements. An object-oriented language such as Java will be used to implement algorithms and further develop general
programming skills. Students will have the option to pay tuition and receive four college credits through Rutgers
University for their CS 112 Data Structures course if all requirements are met.
Available as a Dual Enrollment course through Rutgers University.
*This course will give AP weight to calculate the student’s GPA.

This course introduces students to programming technologies, design, and development related to mobile applications.
Topics include accessing device capabilities, industry standards, operating systems, and programming for mobile
applications. The main thrust of the course will be “hands-on” experience developing apps that address students’
everyday lives and needs.
Computer Repair and Technical Support I Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
This hands-on PC repair course familiarizes students with standard PC hardware and software systems. Students
dismantle and reassemble a PC, learning about industry-standard hardware systems and components. Students will also
be introduced to and practice standard PC support techniques with special regard to personal safety and system reliability.
Diagnosis and resolution of common hardware failures and configuration/compatibility issues are stressed, and students
are given hands-on practice using proven troubleshooting techniques to solve problems.
Computer Repair and Technical Support II Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Computer Applications & Keyboarding and Technical Support I

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
Computer Repair and Technical Support II Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Computer Applications & Keyboarding and Technical Support I
This hands-on course builds on the knowledge acquired in Computer Repair and Technical Support I and provides an
advanced understanding of hardware, networks, and operating systems in a PC environment. Diagnosis and resolution of
common software failures are stressed, and students are given hands-on practice using proven troubleshooting
techniques to solve problems. Participants learn to install, configure, upgrade, and repair an operating system, with a
particular focus on managing its software configuration. Students will also explore the career of the PC technician and
opportunities in this field.
Computer Science I (Computer Science Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5

Prerequisite - None
This elective course will introduce students to programming syntax and algorithm design by using various drag-and-drop
development kits.

isite - None
This elective course will introduce students to programming syntax and algorithm design by using various drag-and-drop
development kits. Through the use of visual Interactive Development Environments and block-based coding, students will
develop and design interactive games, widgets, and app elements while gaining an understanding of basic computer
programming principles such as decision-making, looping, variable use, and an object-oriented approach to
problem-solving. Although the emphasis will be on graphically based development environments, a connection to
text-based coding will be used throughout the course to help students transition to text-based languages.

This elective course will introduce students to text-based programming syntax and Object-Oriented programming.
Students will develop object-oriented programming design skills while creating Classes, Methods, and Functions within
various IDEs. Students will create and utilize variables, arrays, decision-making, and loops in their programming.

Cybersecurity I (Computer Science Career Concentration course) Credits 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Prerequisites - None
This course is designed for students who are interested in exploring careers in Cybersecurity. The focus of instruction will
include the implementation and monitoring of security on network and computer systems. Students will investigate
strategies to identify and protect against security threats such as hackers, eavesdropping, and network attacks. The
basics of cryptography and logic reasoning will be explored. Hands-on labs in a cyber range provide practice in
configuring and mitigating system vulnerabilities.

Cybersecurity II (Computer Science Career Concentration course) Credits 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Prerequisites - Cybersecurity I
This course is designed for students interested in exploring careers in Cybersecurity and its foundations. Building on the
concepts from Cybersecurity I, instruction will focus on vulnerabilities in digital systems and methods for implementing
defenses. Students will apply tools to identify attack vectors and mitigate vulnerabilities. Advanced forms of cryptography
will be explored, along with their role in securing remote transactions. Hands-on labs conducted in a cyber range provide
practice in configuring and mitigating system vulnerabilities.

Data Science (Computer Science Career Concentration course) Credits 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - AP Computer Science Principles OR Computer Science II
With data permeating every aspect of life, the ability to analyze datasets is an important skill for exploring real-world
problems and inquiries. This course equips students with the essential skills of a data scientist, including data collection,
cleaning, transformation, analysis, and visualization. Students will learn to examine and analyze data to form conclusions.
The Python programming environment is used to write and run programs using Python libraries to develop algorithms, tell
data stories, and build statistical models. The course topics include data gathering, importing, filtering, cleaning,
visualizing, trend analysis, correlation analysis, sorting, and bias.

BRHS Program of Studies page 36
Science Department Course Descriptions
Advanced Placement Biology Credits: 6
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite - Successful completion of Biology, completed or concurrently enrolled in Chemistry
The Advanced Placement Biology course is designed to be equivalent to the general biology course usually taken during
the first year of college and prepares students for the College Board Advanced Placement Examination. The advanced
placement program provides an opportunity for secondary school students to pursue and earn college-level credit. It is
highly recommended that the students take the Advanced Placement Biology exam in the spring. Summer assignment
required.
Advanced Placement Chemistry Credits: 6
Grades 11, 12
The Advanced Placement Chemistry course is designed to be the equivalent of the general chemistry course usually taken
during the first year of college.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
Advanced Placement Chemistry course is designed to be the equivalent of the general chemistry course usually taken
during the first year of college. The advanced placement program allows secondary school students to pursue and earn
college-level credit for college-level coursework. It is highly recommended that the students take the Advanced Placement
Chemistry exam in the spring. Students should have completed two years of academic mathematics. Successful
completion of Honors Chemistry is also highly recommended. Summer assignment required.
Advanced Placement Environmental Science Credits: 6
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisites - Completion of Biology and Chemistry, and successful completion of at least one year of Algebra.
The goal of the AP Environmental Science course is to provide students with the scientific principles, concepts, and
methodologies required to understand the interrelationships of the natural world, to identify and analyze environmental
problems, both natural and human-made, to evaluate the relative risks associated with these problems, and to examine
alternative solutions for resolving and/or preventing them.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
human-made, to evaluate the relative risks associated with these problems, and to examine
alternative solutions for resolving and/or preventing them. The course focuses on the “real science” behind environmental
problems. Laboratory and field studies are an important element of the course. Summer assignment required.
Advanced Placement Physics 1 Credits: 6
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite – successful completion of Chemistry (B or better) or Academic Physics (B or better) and Pre-Calculus (B or
better). Alternatively, students who have not completed Pre-Calculus should, at a minimum, be concurrently enrolled in
Honors Pre-Calculus and have earned a grade of A- or better in their most recent math course. Trigonometric functions
will be utilized; students should have a working knowledge of trigonometry.
AP Physics 1 is equivalent to a first-semester college course in algebra-based physics. The course requires students to
develop a deep understanding of the following topics: Newtonian mechanics (including rotational dynamics and angular
momentum); work, energy, and power; mechanical waves and sound; electric charge, electric force, and basic electric
circuits (including resistors).

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
r
momentum); work, energy, and power; mechanical waves and sound; electric charge, electric force, and basic electric
circuits (including resistors). Summer assignment required.
Advanced Placement Physics 2 Credits: 6
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite – successful completion of Honors Physics (B+ or better), AP Physics 1 or AP Physics C
AP Physics 2 is equivalent to a second-semester college course in algebra-based physics. The course covers fluid
mechanics, thermodynamics, electricity and magnetism, optics, and atomic and nuclear physics. Incoming students
should have a solid understanding of AP Physics 1 topics. Summer assignment required.

BRHS Program of Studies page 37
Advanced Placement Physics C Credits 6
Grade 12
Prerequisite - Calculus (a first-year Physics course is recommended but not required)
The Advanced Placement Physics C-level course is rigorous and in-depth, covering only “Newtonian Mechanics” and
“Electricity and Magnetism.” Calculus methods are used wherever appropriate to formulate physical principles and apply
them to physical problems. Strong emphasis is placed on solving a variety of challenging problems, some of which require
calculus.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
inciples and apply
them to physical problems. Strong emphasis is placed on solving a variety of challenging problems, some of which require
calculus. The Physics C course is the first part of a sequence that, in college, is sometimes a very intensive one-year
course but often extends over one and one-half to two years, with a laboratory component. Summer assignment
required.
Biology (A) Credits: 6
Grade 9
Biology is a field of science that relates primarily to life processes and living things. It seeks to discover and describe all
the phenomena associated with living things and with the state of being alive. It develops in us a realization of the
unifying principles of plant and animal development and behavior, the awareness of the order underlying the growth and
structure of plants and animals, and the discovery of the sensitive balances that regulate the lives of all organisms,
including man.
Biology (Honors) Credits: 6
Grade 9
This course is a comprehensive, investigation-oriented introduction to biology and is intended to prepare students to
better understand the development of life processes and living forms through time.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

better understand the development of life processes and living forms through time. Major emphasis is placed on biological
themes and key concepts within the framework of scientific inquiry. Laboratory work is an integral part of the course, thus
encouraging direct participation and insight into scientific methodology. This course is designed for the accelerated
student.

This course will establish the fundamentals of chemistry so that further study in this and related fields may be pursued

It meets for six periods per week, allowing carefully controlled experimentation in a laboratory setting. This is a
challenging course that requires excellent Math and study skills.

BRHS Program of Studies page 38
Chemistry SI (A) Credits: 6
Grade 10
Prerequisite - Successful completion of Biology
This course is for Multilingual Learners and will meet both science content objectives and language objectives in a
differentiated, sheltered-instruction learning environment. This course will establish the fundamentals of chemistry so that
further study in this and related fields may be pursued more readily. It meets for six periods per week, allowing the
performance of carefully controlled experimentation in a laboratory setting. Strong mathematical skills are highly
correlated with success in chemistry.
Conceptual Physics (A) Credits: 6
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite – Successful completion of Chemistry
Conceptual Physics has a hands-on, minds-on, project-based focus that illustrates Physics concepts while using algebra,
geometry, analysis, and critical thinking to quantify them.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
-on, minds-on, project-based focus that illustrates Physics concepts while using algebra,
geometry, analysis, and critical thinking to quantify them. The course requires students to develop a deep conceptual
understanding of the following topics: Newtonian mechanics, work, energy, and power; mechanical waves and sound;
electric charge, electric force, and basic electric circuits.
This course emphasizes scientific exploration and experimentation. Students are expected to engage in classroom
experiences by asking more questions, exploring and discussing possible solutions, investigating science concepts, using
argumentation, and being fully active in the learning process. Additionally, this course incorporates important engineering,
technology, and innovation principles for all students.

Grades 11, 12

The study of physics is necessary for scientific research and inquiry in fields such as engineering and medicine.

The study of physics is necessary for scientific research and inquiry in fields such as engineering and medicine. This
course emphasizes both a conceptual understanding of physical relationships and the ability to analyze them
quantitatively, which requires a significantly more advanced use of algebra than in chemistry and right-angle trigonometry.
Students not proficient in right-angle trigonometry will be required to learn the skills during the class in order to be
successful in the course. Laboratory experimentation in the course is an important part of understanding concepts and
quantitative relationships.

Honors Physics is an in-depth course involving the observation/description, data gathering/analysis, and concept
formation of physical phenomena.

Earth & Space Science (A) Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite – Successful completion of 2 years of science
This course is designed as a survey of the sciences studied when dealing with the forces that affect our planet. The
branches of science that will be covered are Astronomy, Geology, Meteorology, and Oceanography. The students will be
introduced to plate tectonics, earthquakes, volcanism, the prediction of systems of weather, the processes of the earth,
planets, and stars with regard to scales of time and size, the geography and geology of ocean basins, chemistry of
seawater, physical dynamics of currents, waves, and tides, coastal processes, and the large scale circulation of the ocean.

Grades 11, 12

An in-depth study of the relationships between chemistry and our biosphere will serve as the foundation of inquiry in this
academic course. Major environmental concerns will be studied, researched, and analyzed throughout the year.

Science Department Electives
Astronomy (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Astronomy begins with the study of galaxies and the distribution of stars, and it develops appropriate units of
measurement for each. The course continues with the principles of mass, gravitation, and motion in the universe, with
consideration of theories of the universe's origin and destiny. Various methods of observing, using a wide variety of
astronomical instruments, are studied.
Exercise Physiology (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite – Academic Biology , Chemistry, or concurrently taking Chemistry
The course begins with a historical perspective on the study of exercise.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
erequisite – Academic Biology , Chemistry, or concurrently taking Chemistry
The course begins with a historical perspective on the study of exercise. Students then move on to homeostasis, ATP
production, and metabolism. Obesity, body composition, and nutrition are studied in relation to exercise. Finally, the
benefits of exercise on the nerve, muscle, and cardiovascular systems are examined. Students are required to exercise
throughout the semester. Four field trips provide additional opportunities for fitness for enrolled students. The topics
covered in this course include:
● The History of Exercise Homeostasis
● Bioenergetics
● Metabolism
● Body Composition
● Nutrition
● Nerve Structure
● Muscular System
● Cardiovascular system
Forensic Science (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisites - Successful completion of Biology . Successful completion OR concurrent enrollment in Chemistry or Physics
This specialized course draws from all disciplines of science to demonstrate the use of scientific knowledge in criminal
investigation. Emphasis will be placed on principles of biology, chemistry, and physics in all topics covered.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
se of scientific knowledge in criminal
investigation. Emphasis will be placed on principles of biology, chemistry, and physics in all topics covered. Subject areas
will include processing a crime scene, eyewitness testimony, forensic anthropology and odontology, blood spatter analysis,
DNA analysis, forensic pathology, fingerprints, impression evidence, hair/fiber evidence, toxicology, questioned
documents, forensic psychology, forensic engineering, firearms evidence, crime scene reconstruction, and jurisprudence.
Emphasis will be placed on laboratory investigation.

Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Biology , Chemistry, or concurrently taking Chemistry
As you go through this course, you will learn about one of the most fascinating subjects possible -- your own body. If you
are to appreciate emerging discoveries in medicine, understand new techniques for detecting and treating disease, and
make use of published facts on how to stay healthy, it is important to learn about the workings of your body and the basic
concepts that form the foundation of Anatomy and Physiology.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

to stay healthy, it is important to learn about the workings of your body and the basic

muscular systems, the nervous system, the integumentary system, and the senses. Students interested in a
second-semester anatomy & physiology course should take Medical Physiology. Laboratory investigations will include
dissections.
Genetics (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite - Biology
This course examines the concepts of genetics & genomes, Mendelian and modern genetics, molecular biology, gene
regulation, biotechnology, evolutionary connections, and biodiversity.

Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Biology , Chemistry, or concurrently taking Chemistry
Human Anatomy & Physiology is a full-year, in-depth, accelerated course that examines the structures and systems of the
body and how they function. Topics covered will include orientational/directional terms of the body, chemistry, cells,
tissues, and the following body systems: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, cardiovascular, lymphatic, immune,
respiratory, digestive, endocrine, and urinary. Diseases and current medical issues will be discussed. This course is
designed for students planning to pursue careers in the healthcare sciences, including nursing, pharmacy, and sports
medicine, as well as those interested in medical school.

Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Successful completion of Biology
This course is designed to introduce students to the diverse marine environments of the world and the organisms that
inhabit them. Emphasis will be placed on the biological features of the oceans, organism-habitat relationships, and
general ecological concepts influencing marine populations and communities. Topics will include learning the ecological
relationships between flora and fauna, understanding the factors that affect marine organisms, and identifying popular
animal species.

Medical Laboratory Technique Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite – Successful completion of 2 years of Science
This specialized science course is designed to provide the knowledge of equipment and techniques that will enable you to
qualify for employment in hospitals, clinical laboratories, and similar technical areas. It will prove valuable to you if you
are considering a career in nursing or allied health fields, or planning to major in biology or pre-med in college. Areas
studied include basic chemistry, solution preparation, and instruction on the operation of medical testing equipment.

BRHS Program of Studies page 41
Medical Physiology (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Biology , Chemistry, or concurrently taking Chemistry
This class will thoroughly cover five major body systems and the associated medical issues. This is a more challenging
class, as it focuses not only on the anatomy & physiology of those body systems but also on medical problems and
treatments (clinical applications). The TLC series "Trauma: Life in the ER" and "Code Blue" will serve as important
teaching tools in this class. This class will cover body systems not covered in General Anatomy & Physiology. Although
General Anatomy & Physiology is NOT a prerequisite, those students interested in an in-depth study of the human body
should take both classes. Topics will include the urinary, immune, cardiovascular, respiratory, and digestive systems, as
well as human development.

Microbiology (H) Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite – Successful completion of Biology and Chemistry (or concurrently enrolled)
Microbiology will discuss the importance of microorganisms not only as causative agents of disease but also as major
contributors to food production, antibiotic manufacture, vaccine development, regulators of ecological systems, and
environmental management. Eukaryo, bacteria, protists, algae, fungi, viruses, viroids, and prions will all be explored.
Biotechnology laboratories and inquiry-based activities will be implemented throughout the course. Science, technology,
engineering, and math will thread throughout the curriculum.
The study of microbiology is extremely rewarding, leading to advances in environmental and human health.

Oceanography/Meteorology (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Take a deep dive into the forces that shape our planet in this dynamic course that bridges the gap between the deep sea
and the open sky. The first half of the semester is devoted to Oceanography, the study of the distribution of land and
water, the ocean-floor topography and sediments, the properties of water and seawater, the characteristics of the open
and coastal oceans, ocean currents, waves, and tides.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

topography and sediments, the properties of water and seawater, the characteristics of the open
and coastal oceans, ocean currents, waves, and tides. The course will then focus on meteorology, covering the

and climate.
Organic & Biochemistry (Honors) Credits: 6
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite - Successful completion of Biology and one full year of Chemistry
This is an advanced chemistry course that will explore special topics not covered in the Chemistry or AP Chemistry
curricula. Students will build on the foundation from their first year of Chemistry and develop an understanding of basic
Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry. This course provides a survey of the structures, properties, and reactions of the
major functional classes of organic and biological molecules, as well as the basic principles of metabolism and molecular
biology.

Available as a Dual Enrollment course through Seton Hall University.

Social Studies
The State of New Jersey requires that a student successfully complete one year of world history/cultures
and a two-year program in U. S. History. Each full-year course earns five credits. The United States History
courses are sequential.
The following flowcharts illustrate possible course sequencing within the department. Students are encouraged to take
the most challenging courses (differentiated by level) in which they are most likely to succeed. Students are
encouraged to read specific course descriptions prior to selection, taking note of prerequisite and
recommendation requirements.

Advanced Placement U.S. History Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - US History I Honors, gade of B- or better
This college-level course takes you through the major political, social, and economic movements that shaped our nation,
starting with the end of USI H. You'll analyze primary sources, debate historical interpretations, and develop the critical
thinking skills colleges are looking for. The course prepares you for the AP exam in May, which can earn you college
credit. Heads up: there's a summer assignment to get you started. Summer assignments are required.
U.S. History I (A) Credits: 5
Grade 9
Ever wonder how the United States went from thirteen colonies to a continental power?
In this course, you'll review what you learned in seventh grade and deepen your understanding of how our nation was
founded, expanded, and transformed from the founding fathers to the Industrial Revolution. You’ll explore how our
nation’s culture, laws, and even our own New Jersey roots have shaped us.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
m the founding fathers to the Industrial Revolution. You’ll explore how our
nation’s culture, laws, and even our own New Jersey roots have shaped us. As an academic-level course, the focus will be
on developing critical thinking skills, civic knowledge, and understanding the big picture.
U.S. History I (Honors) Credits: 5
Grade 9
This honors track is ideal for students seeking a challenge to develop their ability to analyze and engage in close reading
of primary and secondary sources. Teachers will help you learn how to make sense and find meaning in a variety of
sources through challenging, concentrated readings and analytical writing assignments that develop your critical thinking
skills through advanced discussions and in-depth analysis. You'll investigate multiple historical perspectives and
interpretations, analyzing why historians might disagree about the past and why these debates matter today. This course
is a prerequisite for the AP US History course and will prepare students for that course or continuing in honors USII.

is a prerequisite for the AP US History course and will prepare students for that course or continuing in honors USII.
U.S. History II (A) Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11
How did we get to where we are today?
You'll explore how America became a superpower, examine major social movements such as the Civil Rights Movement,
and connect historical events to issues we still debate today. You'll see patterns in how political, economic, and social
forces have shaped modern America. We examine the significant changes: the Roaring Twenties, the Cold War, the Civil
Rights movement, and current global issues. It’s history that feels relevant to the world you see on the news every day.
U.S. History II (Honors) Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11
This honors course covers the same era as U.S. History II, but challenges you to think like a historian. You'll read
competing interpretations of major events and engage in deeper analysis through extensive reading and writing.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
to think like a historian. You'll read
competing interpretations of major events and engage in deeper analysis through extensive reading and writing. Ideal for
students seeking to develop advanced analytical skills and explore American history from multiple perspectives.
World Civilizations II (A) Credits: 5
Grade 12
This is a state-mandated, full-year course required of students in the Classes of 2027 and 2028. Students must
successfully complete the course to earn credit toward the state-mandated graduation requirement. The focus of the first
year of this course was Western civilization. The focus of World Civilizations II is non-Western civilizations from 1350
through contemporary issues. Students will gain an understanding of the geography, environment, economic
innovation/technology, history, and culture of major non-Western civilizations.

Social Studies Electives
The Social Studies Department offers elective courses to meet student needs and interests.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
BRHS Program of Studies page 44
Social Studies Electives
The Social Studies Department offers elective courses to meet student needs and interests. Students are welcome to take
any or all of these courses, but are reminded that none of these courses meets the New Jersey State Mandate for three
years of Social Studies.
Advanced Placement European History Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
AP European History is a college-level course for students interested in how Europe’s past has shaped the modern world.
You will explore major political revolutions, cultural movements, economic changes, and social transformations from the
Renaissance (1450) to the present. The course balances learning historical facts with developing strong analytical and
writing skills as you evaluate primary sources and competing historical interpretations. Students are expected to take the
AP exam in May.
Advanced Placement Human Geography Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Why do cities form where they do? How does where you live shape who you are?
This College Board course connects geographic concepts to real-world scenarios you see every day.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
they do? How does where you live shape who you are?
This College Board course connects geographic concepts to real-world scenarios you see every day. You'll investigate
population trends, explore diverse cultural landscapes, and identify spatial patterns that explain how our world works.
Through analyzing maps, charts, graphs, infographics, and images, you'll develop skills to spot patterns and trends in
data. From bustling cities to remote rural areas, you'll examine how people interact with their environment and shape the
spaces they inhabit.
Advanced Placement Government and Politics, US Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Want to understand how American politics really works?
This college-level course goes beyond the headlines to give you an analytical perspective on U.S.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
t to understand how American politics really works?
This college-level course goes beyond the headlines to give you an analytical perspective on U.S. government and politics.
You'll study the Constitution's framework, examine how political parties and interest groups influence policy, analyze how
the media shapes public opinion, and investigate how the three branches of government function (and sometimes clash).
The course covers six major areas: constitutional foundations, political beliefs and behaviors, political parties and media,
national government institutions, public policy, and civil rights and civil liberties. You'll learn to interpret political events
and prepare for the AP exam.
Advanced Placement Psychology Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Why do people act the way they do?
This course introduces you to psychology as a science, tracing its evolution from 19th-century philosophy to modern
research methods. You'll explore the major schools of psychological thought and discover how psychologists use
experiments and research to answer questions about behavior.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

ll explore the major schools of psychological thought and discover how psychologists use
experiments and research to answer questions about behavior. The course covers diverse fields within psychology—from
clinical to cognitive to social—and emphasizes the importance of ethics in both research and practice. You'll learn about
experimental methods, proper sampling, and control procedures. Perfect preparation for the AP exam and understanding
human behavior.
Advanced Social Studies-Abnormal Psychology* Credits: 2.5
Grade 12
Prerequisite - Minimum course grade of B- in AP Psychology
This advanced course explores psychological disorders and patterns of maladaptive behavior through biological,
psychological, and social perspectives. Students will study the causes, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of disorders
such as anxiety, mood disorders, schizophrenia, substance abuse, and developmental disorders. Ethical and legal issues in
mental health are also examined.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
as anxiety, mood disorders, schizophrenia, substance abuse, and developmental disorders. Ethical and legal issues in
mental health are also examined. Students will gain insight into how mental health challenges impact their everyday lives
and develop empathy and awareness regarding mental health issues.
*This course carries AP weight for GPA calculation.

BRHS Program of Studies page 45
Advanced Social Studies-Social Psychology* Credits: 2.5
Grade 12
Prerequisite - Minimum course grade of B- in AP Psychology
Ever wonder why you act differently in groups than alone? Why do people conform? What makes someone
help a stranger?
This seminar-style course explores how people think, feel, and act in social situations. You'll examine fascinating
experiments and current research on conformity, obedience, prosocial and antisocial behavior, stereotypes and prejudice,
persuasion, and interpersonal attraction. Through regular discussions and writing, you'll develop skills to recognize and
respond to the social situations you encounter daily.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
nal attraction. Through regular discussions and writing, you'll develop skills to recognize and
respond to the social situations you encounter daily. The course uses a research-based approach to understanding human

American Law (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Know your rights—and your responsibilities.
This semester elective gives you practical insight into how the legal system actually works. Through court decisions and
case studies, you'll explore the mechanics of the judicial process. You'll study criminal law (from investigations to trials),
civil law (lawsuits and disputes), tort law (when someone wrongs you), contract law (making agreements legally binding),
consumer law (your rights as a buyer), juvenile law (how the system treats minors), and constitutional law (your
fundamental rights).

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
gally binding),
consumer law (your rights as a buyer), juvenile law (how the system treats minors), and constitutional law (your
fundamental rights). Perfect for anyone interested in law, criminal justice, or just being an informed citizen.
Civics (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Have you ever wondered how our democratic republic works or how you could be more involved in it?
This one-semester course investigates a basic understanding of politics and government at the federal, state, & local
levels. We’ll also dive into your role in government, and you’ll learn how you can participate in it and change the things
you want to change.
Global Studies (A) Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Climate change doesn't stop at borders. Neither do economic crises, energy shortages, or human rights violations. This
full-year course examines the major issues facing humanity today—problems that transcend national boundaries and
require global solutions. You'll explore economic development, media literacy, environmental concerns, energy
alternatives, national security, and human rights from an international perspective.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
omic development, media literacy, environmental concerns, energy
alternatives, national security, and human rights from an international perspective. The course helps you understand how
interconnected our world has become and how global issues affect your daily life.
Global Studies (Honors) Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Ready to tackle the world's biggest challenges?
This honors course adopts a problem-solving approach to global issues, including economic development, environmental
crises, international security, and envisioning alternative futures. You'll engage in extensive reading and writing, conduct
individual research, and critically examine the media. This course challenges you to think critically about complex global
problems and develop solutions. Expect rigorous analysis and the opportunity to explore topics that matter to you.
Human Development (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
The ultimate "know yourself" course. This introduction to psychology focuses on how you grow, how you learn, and what
makes up your personality.

The ultimate "know yourself" course. This introduction to psychology focuses on how you grow, how you learn, and what

BRHS Program of Studies page 46
Sociology (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
In this semester elective, you will analyze the social structure of American society. You’ll study the tools of sociology and
the socialization process. In the most basic sense, you’ll be looking at why we do the things we do. You’ll see how your
everyday behaviors are socially constructed and vary by culture. Topics such as crime, deviance, racial, ethnic, and gender
issues, and family are all examined.
Women’s Studies: Evolving Image of Women (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Explore how gender roles were built—and how they were smashed.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

amined.
Women’s Studies: Evolving Image of Women (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Explore how gender roles were built—and how they were smashed. From the fight for the vote to modern struggles with
body image and the media, this course challenges preconceived notions about what it means to be a woman in today's
world.
Women’s Studies: Social Change, Changing Gender Roles (A) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
The 20th century changed everything. This course examines how major events (such as world wars and economic shifts)
reshaped women's roles in families and workplaces worldwide. It’s an eye-opening look at how much—and how little—has
changed.

The Multilingual Education Department recognizes that learning an additional language is an asset, as it fosters academic
success, enhances communication, offers cognitive benefits, and expands employment opportunities.

tional language is an asset, as it fosters academic

Changing a World Language Honors Course:
It is suggested that the World Language program be started at level II if the language was previously studied and
successfully completed in 7th and 8th grade. The study of a world language is cumulative, and the honors curriculum is
the most rigorous.
Dropping a world language honors course is a serious decision and can be made only with the written permission of the
parent/guardian and after consultation with the School Counselor and department supervisor. Students who drop a world
language honors course for whatever reason and request to take an academic-level course instead must make a lateral
move. They cannot change course levels.

The Advanced Placement Chinese, French, German, Italian, or Spanish Language course is intended for those students
who wish to take the Advanced Placement Examination in May. The course is designed to develop students’ knowledge of
Chinese, French, German, Italian, or Spanish, equivalent to a third-year college course.

Chinese, French, German, Italian, or Spanish, equivalent to a third-year college course. Great emphasis is placed on the
mastery of interpretive (listening and reading), interpersonal (person-to-person), and presentational (speaking and writing
for an audience) skills.
A continuing focus will be the development of vocabulary and idiomatic language usage, the advancement of cultural
knowledge and skills, and the ability to communicate actively in a variety of oral and written forms. A comprehensive
review of grammar/characters (Chinese) is also included. Each curriculum provides a challenging program that may lead
to college credit or to a student's acceptance into an advanced program as a college freshman. Summer assignment
required.

Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite - Latin IV
The Advanced Placement Latin course is intended for students who wish to take the Advanced Placement – Virgil
Examination in May. Students will engage in a close reading of the Aeneid. They will improve their vocabulary and
translation skills and learn how to interpret and analyze a masterpiece of literature.

improve their analytical and higher-order thinking skills, which will serve them well in college and in their future
workplace. The curriculum offers a challenging program that may lead to college credit or result in a student being
accepted into an advanced program as a college freshman. Summer assignment required.

Program Sequence
Students are grouped in sections of ESL as outlined below, using their WIDA English Proficiency Level (ELP) for

The goal of the high school ESL program is to meet the needs of culturally and linguistically diverse, multilingual learners
(MLs) by providing effective instruction that increases English language proficiency and student academic achievement.
The curriculum has been developed using the WIDA Standards Framework and relevant content standards.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
ge proficiency and student academic achievement.
The curriculum has been developed using the WIDA Standards Framework and relevant content standards. Students are
assessed annually using a WIDA assessment to monitor language growth and determine when they are ready to exit the
program, in accordance with the process outlined in N.J.A.C. 6A:15, Bilingual Education. Time in the program may vary
from student to student. Students are considered for exit when they receive a WIDA score of 4.5 or higher.
“Multilingual Learner” or “ML” means a student whose primary language is not English, who is identified through the
process set forth in N.J.A.C. 6A:15, Bilingual Education, and who is developing proficiency in multiple languages (for
example, English and a primary language). The term is synonymous with “English learner” or “English language learner.”

Physical Education I, II, III, IV Credits: 3.75 yr
Required (all students)
The Bridgewater-Raritan Physical Education Department is committed to educating our students to become lifelong
learners of wellness by participating in activities that promote good health, appropriate social skills, and physical fitness
both in and outside of educational settings. Bridgewater-Raritan students must be provided with opportunities to engage
in a physical education program that develops the necessary skills to participate in individual and team sports and fosters
a positive attitude towards physical activities.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
program that develops the necessary skills to participate in individual and team sports and fosters
a positive attitude towards physical activities. Students will participate in a coeducational physical education program that
incorporates large-group, team, dual, cooperative, and individual activities.
In ninth and tenth grade, students will be exposed to various activities, including group initiatives, a low-confidence
course, fitness, field hockey, basketball, floor hockey, lacrosse, soccer, softball, table tennis, team handball, and volleyball.
In grades eleven and twelve, students have the opportunity to participate in a variety of activities that include aerobics,
badminton, basketball, football, hacky sack, pickleball, soccer, softball, table tennis, punch ball, tennis, ultimate Frisbee,
volleyball, weight training, yoga, and the high confidence course.

Instead of Physical Education III or IV, students can apply to the PE Partners program. P.E. Partners is a collaboration

Health I Credits: 1.25
Required—Grade 9
Ninth-grade Health allows students to investigate teenage behaviors, human physiological functions, and the avoidance of
sexually transmitted diseases, including HIV/AIDS. In furthering the wellness concept, students review balanced nutrition
and the importance of physical fitness as integral to overall well-being. Other units of study during ninth-grade health
include Body Systems, Family Living, Alcohol –Tobacco, and Other Drugs, Diseases, Sexuality, Abstinence Education, and
Effective Communication Skills.
Health II Credits: 1.25
Required—Grade 10—Driver Education Theory
Tenth-grade Health focuses on the automobile in modern life and aims to develop mature attitudes and proper habits for
safe driving.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
Education Theory
Tenth-grade Health focuses on the automobile in modern life and aims to develop mature attitudes and proper habits for
safe driving. It is an aid in preparing for the written examination given by the State Department of Motor Vehicles.
Included in this course will be a continued study of drugs, alcohol, and tobacco, along with their relationship to the
operation of a motor vehicle.
Health III Credits: 1.25
Required—Grade 11
Eleventh-grade health includes topics such as Family Living, Mental, Emotional, and Social Health, and Human Growth
and Development. Students will examine societal influences and the factors that weaken or strengthen relationships.
Through a variety of structured classroom experiences, students will learn problem-solving strategies to use in different
situations. (Topics can include healthy or unhealthy relationships, death, harassment, drugs, and diseases). Students will
also explore personality development and feelings of positive self-worth.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
althy relationships, death, harassment, drugs, and diseases). Students will
also explore personality development and feelings of positive self-worth. Through a variety of exercises involving the
decision-making process, students will increase their coping skills in such areas as stress, peer pressure, substance abuse,
suicide, and risk situations of sexual assault and abuse.
Health IV Credits: 1.25
Required—Grade 12
Senior health units include Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation, Family Living, Human Growth and Development, and Disease
Prevention. During CPR, students will be given a practical approach to life-saving techniques and methods to avoid
injuries and illnesses. (Students also have the opportunity to become certified in CPR). During Family Life and Human
Growth and Development, students will recognize the development of a child from conception to birth. Students will also
study prenatal and infant care, budgeting for the childbirth process, and other responsibilities of parenthood and
child-rearing.

of musical theater from its origins to contemporary works.

hat makes a live performance look, sound, and feel incredible?

After selecting material, students oversee productions from start to finish, culminating in a public presentation of one-act

Why do some songs get stuck in your head—and others give you chills?

popular hits.

BRHS Program of Studies page 55
Business & Marketing
The Department recognizes that citizens in the 21st Century need diverse knowledge, skills, and competencies. As a
result, the Business & Marketing Department provides students with a dynamic experience in technology and business
from seventh grade through their senior year.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
Business & Marketing Department provides students with a dynamic experience in technology and business
from seventh grade through their senior year. The Business & Marketing Department believes that, at the middle school
level, the program prepares students with the skills necessary to succeed in their secondary education. At the secondary
level, the Business & Marketing Department's focus shifts to a philosophy of technology and business integration that
equips students with the skills to succeed in the rapidly changing technological world in which they live.
The staff of the Business & Marketing Department believes that offering a unique blend of curricula will allow students to
gain the diverse knowledge, skills, and competencies necessary to succeed in the 21st Century. The Business & Marketing
staff accepts all students, regardless of individual skill or prerequisite knowledge, and strives to stimulate, encourage, and
support their growth in technology and business. The Business & Marketing Department works hard to ensure the
educational environment is an open and inviting place where everyone feels safe and encouraged to learn.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
arketing Department works hard to ensure the
educational environment is an open and inviting place where everyone feels safe and encouraged to learn. Innovative
curricula allow students to gain knowledge that is then applied, adapted, and used to encourage other students to
develop a desire to become lifelong learners in our technological society.
All courses in the Business & Marketing Department are intended to expose students to real-world situations while
encouraging them to question and analyze their knowledge and understanding of the world. Students are challenged to
utilize technology to be creative problem-solvers while strengthening the skills necessary to become leaders in their
schools, communities, and, ultimately, in the ever-changing technological world.

This subject is the introductory course in a recommended two-year sequence that introduces students to the basic
principles, concepts, and procedures of accounting, including computerized accounting. Students will go through the
entire accounting cycle, from analyzing and recording transactions to preparing financial statements.

erized accounting. Students will go through the

and concepts as they apply to proprietorships, partnerships, and corporations. Students will also learn to interpret and
analyze financial reports.

Advanced Placement Business with Personal Finance (Business Career Concentration Course) Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite – Minimum grade of “A-” in Introduction to Business, ability to read a college-level textbook, and express
themselves clearly in writing
Get ready to build the skills you need to run a business and manage your own money!
AP Business with Personal Finance is a challenging, year-long course that dives deep into what it takes to succeed in the
modern economy. It’s essentially a college-level introduction to business, designed to give you a serious head start. In
this class, you won't just memorize definitions. You'll tackle real-world business challenges, analyze case studies, and get
hands-on with project-based learning.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
ou won't just memorize definitions. You'll tackle real-world business challenges, analyze case studies, and get
hands-on with project-based learning. You will explore key business fields, including:
● Entrepreneurship: Learn how to spot opportunities and launch your big idea.
● Marketing: Discover the secrets behind branding and connecting with customers.
● Finance & Accounting: Master how money flows in a company and how to read the numbers.
● Management: Develop leadership skills and learn how organizations make smart decisions.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

nal Finance
This AP course aims to give students a thorough understanding of the principles of economics that apply to an economic
system as a whole. The curriculum emphasizes the study of national income and price determination and develops
students' familiarity with economic performance measures, economic growth, and international economics. Coverage of
these concepts provides students with the foundation for a thorough understanding of macroeconomics in a global
context.
Advanced Placement Microeconomics (Business Career Concentration Course) Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite – Final grade of “A-“ or better in Integrated Algebra II/Data Science, OR Algebra II, OR a final grade of “B-“
or better in Honors Algebra II, OR successful completion of AP Business with Personal Finance
This college-level course is an in-depth study of how individuals, firms, and organizational structures make economic
decisions. Areas of study will include the following: demand and supply analysis, allocation of goods and services, factors
of productivity, and the distribution of goods and services throughout the economy.

emphasis is placed on reasoned, logical arguments using economics as a method and model for decision-making.
Business & Criminal Law (Business Career Concentration Course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
The legal environment of Business and Criminal Law overlaps quite frequently. This course will provide an overview of
both areas, introducing students to various aspects of business and criminal law for institutions and individuals in society,
as well as general legal and social law. Throughout, emphasis is placed on the basic theory of business and criminal law,
the relationship between doctrines, and the various justifications for the imposition of punishment.

Career Exploration and Awareness (Business Career Concentration Course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
This course allows students to explore career opportunities and prepare themselves for employment. Students will take
self-assessments and study employment trends to help them focus on career considerations. They will explore various
tools to evaluate and research careers, colleges, and trade schools. This class will examine skills and professional conduct
required in the workplace. Students will explore employment laws and employee benefits, salary, and taxes. Students will
also create a customized resume and cover letter to prepare for and participate in a mock interview.

Students are allowed to analyze and assess economic cause/effect relationships. Students will take a “hands-on” approach

BRHS Program of Studies page 57
Entrepreneurship & Business Management (Business Career Concentration Course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite – Introduction to Business
This course will focus on the steps to take when creating a business, including recognizing and developing the opportunity
into a small business. By developing a business idea, students will work collaboratively to write a business plan and pitch
it to the other class members.
Financial Planning (Business Career Concentration Course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 11, 12
This course was developed to give high school students the knowledge and skills to manage their finances. Students will
learn to evaluate the risks and rewards of financial decisions.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

high school students the knowledge and skills to manage their finances. Students will
learn to evaluate the risks and rewards of financial decisions. This class covers credit management, credit laws, debt and

mutual funds, index funds, ETFs, and basic retirement planning.
International Business (Business Career Concentration Course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
This course will provide students with a foundation for understanding international business and the various aspects of
conducting business in a global economy. It will help students understand the customs of people from different
geographic, cultural, racial, and ethnic backgrounds around the world. Students will study the technological advances of
the Internet and e-commerce that enable the delivery of goods and services worldwide. This course prepares students to
work, live, and think in a global environment.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
This course is designed to introduce students to business and financial literacy.

This course is designed to introduce students to business and financial literacy. Students will be exposed to business
terminology, concepts, practices, and personal finance. Students will establish a basic business vocabulary and foster
critical thinking while evaluating current business practices. Topics covered in this class include basic economics, types of
business ownership, owning and operating a business, business ethics, international business, credit, budgeting, banking,
and investment strategies.

This course will provide students with the opportunity to study practical leadership skills to address problems in their
community. Participants will be exposed to the concepts and principles of philanthropic work and the challenge of
sustained service.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - None (Suggested: Introduction to Business)
This course builds core marketing skills and analyzes the evolving social media landscape. Students are introduced to
such topics as Advertising, Marketing Plans, International Marketing, the Marketing Mix, Consumer Behavior, and the
Product Life Cycle. Students also learn how Marketers use Social Media platforms to market products and services.

Grade 12
Ever wondered how you’re actually supposed to be an adult? This senior-only course is designed to teach you the real-life

Culinary Arts
Welcome to the Culinary Arts program—where food, creativity, and hands-on learning come together! Whether you’re a
beginner or an experienced home cook, our classes give you the chance to learn real kitchen skills, explore new flavors,
and discover what makes the culinary world so exciting.
You’ll spend plenty of time cooking in our fully equipped kitchens, practicing techniques, trying new recipes, and creating
dishes you can be proud of.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
u’ll spend plenty of time cooking in our fully equipped kitchens, practicing techniques, trying new recipes, and creating
dishes you can be proud of. Along the way, you’ll learn important skills like teamwork, communication, time management,
and problem-solving—skills that matter both in and out of the kitchen.
Start with Introduction to Culinary Arts, where you’ll learn the essentials of cooking, safety, and kitchen tools. From
there, you can choose from a variety of courses that fit your interests:
● Baking & Pastry Arts – Dive into breads, pastries, cakes, and more while learning the science behind baking.
● Cooking Across America – Explore iconic foods from different U.S. regions and learn what makes each style
unique.
● Culinary Nutrition (Honors) – Blend cooking with food science and nutrition while creating meals focused on
health and wellness.
● International Foods – Travel the world through food as you prepare dishes from a variety of cultures and
cuisines.
Each class gives you more freedom to be creative, try new techniques, and expand your knowledge of the culinary world.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12

If you loved the baking portion of Intro to Culinary, this course takes it to the next level. You’ll dive deeper into the
science of baking—learning what ingredients do, why measurements matter, and how to follow (and eventually adapt)
recipes with confidence. Through hands-on baking labs, you’ll practice techniques, learn about design and presentation,
and create a wide variety of baked goods.

Cooking Across America Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Introduction to Culinary Arts
Take a culinary road trip across the United States! In this course, you’ll explore different regions of the country and the
foods that make each one unique. Through hands-on cooking labs, you’ll learn about the cultural, historical, and creative
influences behind American regional cuisine. You’ll continue to build your skills in planning, preparation, presentation, and
teamwork while having more freedom to experiment and get creative in the kitchen.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Get ready to jump into the world of cooking! In this course, you’ll learn the essential skills needed in the kitchen—from

BRHS Program of Studies page 60
Textile Arts
Step into a world of creativity, craftsmanship, and design.
The Fashion & Fiber Arts program invites students to bring ideas to life through hands-on making, technical skill
development, and original design.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
.
The Fashion & Fiber Arts program invites students to bring ideas to life through hands-on making, technical skill
development, and original design. Whether you are new to sewing or already interested in design-focused creative work,
these courses help you build practical skills while expressing your personal style.
Students work in a collaborative studio environment, planning, creating, and refining projects using industry-relevant tools
and techniques. Across the program, students learn to select and work with fabrics and fibers, read and modify patterns,
safely operate sewing machines and sergers, and apply both technical and creative problem-solving skills. The program
also explores how clothing, textiles, and wearable art connect to culture, function, and modern design.
Students typically begin with Sewing Technique, where they develop foundational construction skills and confidence
using tools and materials.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
design.
Students typically begin with Sewing Technique, where they develop foundational construction skills and confidence
using tools and materials. From there, students may expand their experience through courses such as Fiber Arts
Technique, which emphasizes textile-based creative processes, and Jewelry Design, which focuses on designing and
constructing wearable art.
The program culminates in Apparel Design & Creation, a comprehensive course in which students design, construct,
and refine original garments from concept to completion. This advanced course emphasizes craftsmanship, design
iteration, and portfolio-quality work.
Throughout the program, students develop independence, creativity, and precision. By the end, students leave with a
strong body of work, practical design skills, and the confidence to pursue personal creative projects or future pathways in
fashion, design, and related fields.
Apparel Design & Creation (Honors) (3D Design & Production Career Concentration course) Credits 5.0
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisites – Two or more of the following courses: Fiber Arts Technique, Jewelry Design, Sewing Technique

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
on course) Credits 5.0
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisites – Two or more of the following courses: Fiber Arts Technique, Jewelry Design, Sewing Technique
Apparel Design & Creation is an advanced, full-year, project-based course focused on the full design and construction
process of original garments. Students move from concept development and sketching through pattern modification,
fabric selection, fitting, and final construction. Emphasis is placed on precision sewing, design iteration, problem-solving,
and understanding how clothing functions in real-world use. Students complete multiple garments and develop a cohesive
body of work suitable for a portfolio. This course is recommended for students considering further study or careers in
fashion, design, or related creative fields.
Fiber Arts Technique (3D Design & Production Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Fiber Arts Technique explores creative and technical approaches to working with textiles beyond traditional garment
sewing. Students investigate fibers, yarns, and fabrics while learning techniques such as embroidery, weaving, dyeing,
surface design, and mixed-media textile work.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

students to express creativity while developing fine-motor and design skills. This course is ideal for students interested in
art, design, or hands-on creative processes using fiber-based materials.
Jewelry Design (3D Design & Production Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Jewelry Design introduces students to the principles of designing and constructing wearable art. Students explore metals,
beads, wire, resin, and alternative materials while learning techniques such as wire wrapping, beading, and basic
fabrication. The course emphasizes design thinking, scale, balance, and personal style, while also addressing
craftsmanship and safe tool use.
