Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Sewing Technique introduces students to the foundational skills and tools used in garment construction and textile
projects. Students learn to use sewing machines and hand-sewing techniques safely and confidently while developing
precision, accuracy, and craftsmanship. Projects focus on essential skills such as measuring, pattern reading, fabric

Advanced Placement Art History (Visual Arts Career Concentration course) Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - none, but Art History (A), World History, or AP World History are good preparations. Students who have
done well in history, literature, and visual art studies are encouraged to enroll.
The AP Art History course is a survey of world history and civilization organized around 250 exemplary works of art and
architecture. Students discover connections across time, culture, and geography by examining these works' form,
function, content, and context.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
t and

Advanced Studio Art (Honors) (Visual Arts Career Concentration course) Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Studio Art or Portfolio Review
Ready to take full ownership of your art and push your ideas as far as they can go?
Advanced Studio Art is a rigorous, student-driven course for artists prepared to work independently and think deeply
about their creative practice. Students engage in all stages of the artistic process—idea development, experimentation,
production, reflection, and presentation—while refining craftsmanship and conceptual thinking. Emphasis is placed on
understanding how ideas, materials, and processes work together to communicate meaning.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

craftsmanship and conceptual thinking. Emphasis is placed on
understanding how ideas, materials, and processes work together to communicate meaning. This course serves as a

for college and scholarship submissions.
Art History (Visual Arts Career Concentration course) Credits: 5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Why have humans always felt the need to make art—and why does it still matter today?
Art History explores painting, sculpture, architecture, and visual traditions from the earliest civilizations to the modern
world. Students examine why art was created, what it meant to the people who made it, and how it reflects cultural
values, beliefs, and identity. Through close analysis and discussion, students develop skills in interpretation and critical
thinking while gaining insight into the human experience.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
rough close analysis and discussion, students develop skills in interpretation and critical

Art History Studio (Visual Arts Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
What if you could learn about historic art—and make it your own?
Art History Studio combines the study of art history with hands-on creative work. Students explore artistic traditions from
across cultures and time periods, then create original artworks inspired by related techniques, ideas, and processes. Each
project encourages connections between history, geography, culture, and personal experience, making this course ideal
for students who enjoy learning through both research and making.
Art Journaling Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Looking for a creative space to explore ideas, emotions, and imagination—no rules required?
Art Journaling introduces students to visual journaling as a personal and expressive art form.

re ideas, emotions, and imagination—no rules required?
Art Journaling introduces students to visual journaling as a personal and expressive art form. Students learn bookmaking
techniques and experiment with a variety of materials to document thoughts, experiences, and ideas through images and
writing. Emphasis is placed on process, reflection, and creative freedom, making this course ideal for students who enjoy
art as a tool for self-expression and storytelling.
Art for Meditation and Relaxation (Visual Arts Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5

Need a creative break from the stress of the school day?
Art for Meditation and Relaxation explores art making as a calming, restorative practice. Drawing inspiration from global
folk art traditions, students work with a variety of materials while learning the elements and principles of art.

What if art could help explain how the world works?
Art and Science explores how visual art can convey scientific ideas, discoveries, and processes. Students investigate
scientific concepts while learning foundational art-making skills, using a variety of materials and approaches.

Ceramics: Hand Building introduces students to the fundamentals of working with clay through sculptural and decorative
forms. Students explore hand-building techniques, surface design, and glazing while developing an understanding of
three-dimensional form.

Ceramics: Wheel Throwing (3D Design & Production or Visual Arts Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Ever wanted to throw clay on a pottery wheel and create something you can actually use?
Ceramics: Wheel Throwing focuses on functional pottery created on the wheel. Students learn traditional throwing
techniques, refine forms, and explore surface design using glazes and finishes. By balancing artistic design with practical
function, students gain both technical skill and an appreciation for how visual art enhances everyday objects.

Drawing and Sketching (Visual Arts Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Want to strengthen the skills that every artist relies on?
Drawing and Sketching focus on developing essential drawing foundations through observation, experimentation, and

image-making. This course is an excellent entry point for students new to art or for those looking to strengthen their
confidence and drawing skills.

preparing students for advanced study in the visual arts.

Love color, experimentation, and expressive painting?
Watercolor Painting introduces students to the unique qualities and techniques of watercolor media. Students explore
color theory, transparency, and composition as they create both realistic and abstract works.

This advanced journalism course builds on skills developed in Journalism and Sports Journalism, while providing students
with greater experience in reporting, writing, editing, and media analysis. Students will produce publishable work while
pitching story ideas, conducting interviews, applying AP Style, collaborating in an editorial environment, and analyzing

American Film Study Credits: 2.5
Grades 11, 12
This course seeks to complement and expand upon the core film units addressed in Language & Literature courses.
Through an academic consideration of the relationship between film and American culture, students will develop the
critical thinking skills needed to meet the demands of contemporary society. Additionally, by studying literary/cinematic
devices and paralleling the formal analysis of print texts, the course will help ensure greater success in the language arts
classroom.
Audio & Music Engineering (Media & Content Creation or Music Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
This course introduces students to the principles of audio recording.

reation or Music Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
This course introduces students to the principles of audio recording. Students will learn to select and set up recording
equipment through guided activities, create multi-track recordings, apply effects, and use advanced mixing and recording
techniques. Students will gain a fundamental understanding of the necessary skills for the audio industry and audio
post-production for video. The curriculum is based on music, audio content production, acoustics, fundamental
engineering science, signal processing hardware and software, electronics, and software engineering.
Digital Fine Arts I (Media & Content Creation or Visual Arts Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Want to create art using the same digital tools professionals use every day?
Digital Fine Arts I introduces students to the exciting intersection of technology, science, and contemporary fine art. The
course expands traditional ideas of fine art, art history, and design by exploring how digital tools shape modern visual
culture.

orary fine art. The

applications while introducing career and vocational pathways in visual communication, design, and digital media.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Ready to take your digital artwork to the next level and build a portfolio that stands out?
Digital Fine Arts II builds on the concepts and skills introduced in Digital Fine Arts I, deepening students’ understanding of
technology-driven art and digital new media. Students refine advanced techniques in Adobe Photoshop, Adobe Illustrator,
Procreate, and PIXLR while creating original, concept-driven artwork. Emphasis is placed on artistic intention, technical
mastery, and contemporary design practices.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Ever wanted to create your own music using technology instead of traditional instruments?
Digital Music Composition uses state-of-the-art music software to explore how music is created, recorded, and produced
in today’s digital world. Students experiment with melody, harmony, rhythm, and texture by layering and manipulating
digitally generated and recorded sounds.

Want to learn how powerful images are made—and tell stories through photography?
Digital Photography explores both the creative and technical sides of image-making. Students learn to control light, focus,
composition, and timing to create compelling photographs for communication and personal expression. The course
introduces basic principles of commercial photography while emphasizing artistic vision.

Production, Podcasting
Step into the director’s chair. The Director’s Workshop is a Media and Communications capstone experience for students
ready to lead big ideas from vision to final cut. Create polished, long-form projects using advanced cinematography,
editing, and sound design. Students manage productions, collaborate like professionals, and build standout portfolio
pieces.

advanced cinematography,

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Design content that people click, scroll through, and interact with. Interactive Content teaches students how to edit
photos, design layouts, and build engaging web-based projects. Learn the basics of image editing, web design, and user
experience while turning creative ideas into polished digital products. Through hands-on projects and collaboration,
students build real skills in visual communication, problem-solving, and digital creativity used across today’s online
platforms.
Introduction to Screenwriting (Media & Content Creation Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 11, 12
This course will enable students to write within the accepted framework for professional film scripts, producing an
act-long script excerpt.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:
s 11, 12
This course will enable students to write within the accepted framework for professional film scripts, producing an
act-long script excerpt. Students will learn to analyze film with an increasingly critical eye, synthesizing previously learned
skills with a more advanced understanding of the art and science of screenwriting. They will then be equipped to apply
these technical and creative processes to their work. The format of the course will include close “readings” of a film and
several film clips concurrently with their scripts; instruction of and practice with script jargon, format, and structure;
experience writing and work-shopping a story pitch; writing a 400-word treatment; visually diagramming existing scripts
as well as original story ideas; and drafting a complete first act of an original screenplay.
Journalism (Media & Content Creation Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
In this course, students will be introduced to newspaper journalism.

Media & Content Creation Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
In this course, students will be introduced to newspaper journalism. Students will learn the specific skills required for
journalistic writing, including fact-gathering, note-taking, objective reporting, interviewing, editing, and proofreading.
Students will read a wide variety of newspapers and literature on how ideas are communicated in order to become more
informed consumers in the media marketplace. Students will have the opportunity to publish their writing in school
periodicals.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Tell stories that matter—and make them cinematic. Narrative & Documentary Production dives into longer-form films,
documentaries, interviews, and episodic content.

hat matter—and make them cinematic. Narrative & Documentary Production dives into longer-form films,

Working in production teams, students manage projects from pre-production to final cut while mastering advanced
techniques and storytelling structure used in professional film and media industries.

BRHS Program of Studies page 68
Podcasting (Media & Content Creation Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Turn your voice into a brand. Podcasting lets students create, record, edit, and publish original podcasts using
professional equipment and industry-standard software. From true crime and sports talk to storytelling and interviews,
students develop real-world media skills in scripting, sound design, editing, and promotion. Perfect for creators who want
to be heard—and remembered—in today’s digital world.

Pop Culture is the culture of the masses. It’s an attitude, style, or trend that becomes popular for a short time.

ry. Credits: 2.5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Pop Culture is the culture of the masses. It’s an attitude, style, or trend that becomes popular for a short time. This
one-semester course will provide you with an understanding of major trends in American art, literature, movies,
music/radio, television, sports, fads, and fashion of the 20th century by immersing you in the experience. You’ll also see
how history can both affect and be affected by the pop culture of the time.
Shorts & Reels (Media & Content Creation Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Shorts and Reels takes short-form video to the next level with advanced editing, transitions, motion graphics, sound
design, and effects. Students design videos for real platforms, real audiences, and real impact—focusing on pacing,
branding, and engagement. Fast-paced, collaborative projects mirror today’s creator economy, helping students think like
editors, storytellers, and digital brands.
Speech Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
An activity-based approach to public speaking, this course provides you with practical lessons on how to communicate
clearly and effectively to diverse audiences.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

ased approach to public speaking, this course provides you with practical lessons on how to communicate
clearly and effectively to diverse audiences. You will learn how to prepare and deliver formal and informal talks. Practice
in voice, diction, and listening will be emphasized throughout the course. Tapes of famous personalities may be used to
illustrate the technique. Finally, you will learn about the similarities and differences in spoken and written English by
writing speeches and dialogues.
Sports Journalism (Media & Content Creation Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Students will learn the specific skills needed for sports journalism writing, including fact-gathering, note-taking, objective
reporting, interviewing, editing, and proofreading. Students will read various sports publications, digital and print, to learn
how ideas are communicated and further hone their writing skills. Sports writing will be analyzed in historical, modern,
and future contexts.

camera, lighting, audio, and editing skills while creating content across multiple genres. From concept to final cut, this
hands-on course blends creativity, collaboration, and visual storytelling—perfect for beginners and experienced creators
ready to make media that stands out.

Design it. Model it. Build it. 3D Prototyping lets students turn ideas into real-world creations using 3D modeling, digital
design, and rapid prototyping tools.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027
content:

physically produced. This hands-on course blends creativity, problem-solving, and tech skills, opening doors to careers in
engineering, product design, architecture, and emerging technologies.
Advanced Mathematics: Topics in Engineering* (Engineering & Robotics Career Concentration course) Credits: 5
Grade 12
Prerequisite - AP Calculus BC OR AP Calculus AB with concurrent enrollment in AP Calculus BC
This course will introduce students to the advanced mathematics and “big ideas” of the standard undergraduate
engineering curriculum. Topics include series expansions, complex numbers, linear algebra and vector spaces, Fourier
analysis, and other advanced calculus methods. The course will consist of an experiential (lab or coding) component in
which students apply their mathematical knowledge to a practical engineering problem.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

se will consist of an experiential (lab or coding) component in

Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite - Principles of Architecture
Design spaces where people live, work, and dream. Architectural Drafting & Design immerses students in professional
Computer-Aided Design (CAD) and 3D modeling tools used by architects today. Students create site plans, floor plans,
elevations, and detailed digital models while blending math, art, and problem-solving. This advanced, project-based
course mirrors real architectural workflows—ideal for creative thinkers pursuing careers in architecture, engineering,
design, or construction.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Think in 3D—and design it for real. Digital 3D Modeling teaches students how to turn ideas and sketches into detailed
digital creations. Students learn to read and create technical drawings, build accurate 3D models, render realistic scenes,
and produce basic animations using industry tools. The course strengthens spatial reasoning, design precision, and
creative problem-solving, while preparing students for careers in engineering, architecture, game design, product
development, and digital media.

Design on a screen. Carve it for real. Digital Fabrication introduces students to CNC technology used in modern
manufacturing and design. Create digital designs and transform them into precise wood, plastic, and metal projects.
Students learn how CNC differs from 3D printing while gaining hands-on experience with industry tools.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
This course will investigate the fundamentals of electrical principles through activities in magnetism, light heat,
elementary electronics, house wiring, and printed circuits. Emphasis is placed on fundamental theory, tools, materials,
equipment maintenance, use, and control of power, meters, computation, and testing. (Algebra I recommended.)

the academic and applied aspects of engineering.

Principles of Engineering is designed to expand students’ foundations in engineering and technology, which began in
Introduction to Engineering (IED). Students will explore various technology systems and manufacturing processes to help
them integrate math, science, and technology and solve practical problems.

Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Students will work in teams to play robotic games, exploring the basic concepts of engineering simple robots. Students
will focus on controlling a robot using driver input and on how to manipulate game objects with claws, arms, and motor
groups.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

imple robots. Students

Programming may be taken in either order.
Robotics Programming (A) (Engineering & Robotics Career Concentration course) Credits: 2.5
Grades 9, 10, 11, 12
Students will work in teams to play robotic games, exploring the basic concepts of programming simple robots. Students
will focus on automating a robot using sensors, logic, and motor groups. Students will also be introduced to the
mechanics of manipulating objects with their robots. Throughout the course, the focus will be on following logical rules
and using trial-and-error to overcome obstacles. This course and Robotics Concept & Design may be taken in either order.

Advanced Placement Research Credits: 5
Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite - Minimum course grade of B- in AP Seminar
AP Research allows students to investigate a topic of personal interest through a year-long research project. Students
design and conduct original research, analyze data, and apply ethical research practices. The course culminates in a
4,000–5,000-word academic paper and an oral presentation with a defense.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

alyze data, and apply ethical research practices. The course culminates in a

Advanced Placement Seminar Credits: 5
Grades 10, 11, 12
Prerequisite – Must meet AP/Honors eligibility requirements in both English and Social Studies
AP Seminar is a college-level course that challenges students to explore real-world issues through research, discussion,
and collaborative work. Collaboration is central to this course: you'll work extensively in teams to investigate topics,
challenge each other's thinking, and create joint presentations. You'll also complete individual work to demonstrate your
personal analytical skills. Through collaborative research projects, presentations, and seminar-style discussions, students
build critical thinking, communication, and leadership skills while also strengthening their reading, writing, and
presentation abilities.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

, students

Grades 11, 12
Prerequisite – Juniors enrolled in a career concentration must have completed at least 10 credits toward their
concentration; all Seniors are eligible
Capstone Experience is a foundational course that engages students in cross-curricular conversations exploring the
complexities of academic and real-world topics related to the concepts they have encountered in high school. Through
this exploration, students learn to synthesize information from multiple sources and to design, plan, and conduct a
semester-long research-based investigation to address a research question of their own interest. Students present their
completed projects at the end of the course.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

esearch-based investigation to address a research question of their own interest. Students present their

Education Internship Credits: 5 or 10
Grade 12 One or Two Semesters
The Education Internship offers Grade 12 students an opportunity to work as an Intern in the Intermediate schools of the
Bridgewater Raritan Regional School District (Hillside & Eisenhower, Grades 5 & 6) and receive either 5 or 10 credits (1
Semester or Full-Year, respectively). This allows students to gain insight into a professional career working with children.
Possible careers include Teachers, Counselors, Therapists/Psychologists, Pediatricians, Nurses, Speech Pathologists,
Physical/Occupational Therapists, and many more.
Requirements include eighty (80) minutes at the assigned school placement and graded assignments throughout the
course.

title: BRHS Course Request Sheets 2026-2027

al Therapists, and many more.
Requirements include eighty (80) minutes at the assigned school placement and graded assignments throughout the

Independent Study Credits: 2.5 - 5
Grades: 9, 10, 11, 12
The Independent Study program at BRHS is intended for individuals seeking intensive study in an academic area not
currently offered in the BRHS curriculum. Independent studies may not replace a course in the BRHS Program of Studies.
Students may seek approval for an Independent Study through the Option II application.
A BRHS-certified faculty member must serve as an advisor and be secured before submitting the Option II application.
The Independent Study advisor is responsible for the final evaluation of the study and must submit verification that
instructional objectives have been met.

ompleted

volunteering. Under Option II, students may apply Service Learning credit toward high school graduation requirements if
